title: RoboLayout: Differentiable 3D Scene Generation for Embodied Agents
summary: Recent advances in vision language models (VLMs) have shown strong potential for spatial reasoning and 3D scene layout generation from open-ended language instructions. However, generating layouts that are not only semantically coherent but also feasible for interaction by embodied agents remains challenging, particularly in physically constrained indoor environments. In this paper, RoboLayout is introduced as an extension of LayoutVLM that augments the original framework with agent-aware reasoning and improved optimization stability. RoboLayout integrates explicit reachability constraints into a differentiable layout optimization process, enabling the generation of layouts that are navigable and actionable by embodied agents. Importantly, the agent abstraction is not limited to a specific robot platform and can represent diverse entities with distinct physical capabilities, such as service robots, warehouse robots, humans of different age groups, or animals, allowing environment design to be tailored to the intended agent. In addition, a local refinement stage is proposed that selectively reoptimizes problematic object placements while keeping the remainder of the scene fixed, improving convergence efficiency without increasing global optimization iterations. Overall, RoboLayout preserves the strong semantic alignment and physical plausibility of LayoutVLM while enhancing applicability to agent-centric indoor scene generation, as demonstrated by experimental results across diverse scene configurations.

AI.tldr: RoboLayout extends LayoutVLM to generate 3D indoor layouts that are not only semantically correct and physically plausible but also explicitly feasible and functional for different embodied agents.
AI.motivation: Existing vision-language models like LayoutVLM can generate semantically coherent and physically plausible indoor layouts from language, but they do not explicitly ensure that the resulting scenes are actually usable by real agents (robots, humans, etc.). In physically constrained indoor environments, objects may be arranged in ways that look reasonable but are not navigable or reachable, limiting applicability to robotics, assistive environments, or agent-centric design. The paper aims to bridge this gap by embedding agent-centric constraints—especially reachability—directly into the layout generation process, and by improving optimization robustness for practical use on diverse agents and environments.
AI.method: The authors build RoboLayout on top of LayoutVLM, retaining its vision-language-based semantic layout capabilities but augmenting it with agent-aware reasoning and a more stable optimization scheme. They introduce an agent abstraction that parameterizes different embodied entities by their physical capabilities (e.g., size, reach, motion constraints). These capabilities are encoded as explicit reachability constraints, which are integrated into a differentiable layout optimization pipeline. During optimization, object positions and orientations are adjusted to satisfy semantic, physical, and agent-reachability constraints simultaneously. Additionally, they propose a local refinement stage that detects problematic object placements and selectively re-optimizes only those regions while freezing the rest of the scene, thereby improving convergence speed and stability without increasing the number of expensive global optimization iterations.
AI.result: Experiments on a variety of indoor scene configurations show that RoboLayout maintains the semantic alignment and physical plausibility achieved by LayoutVLM while producing layouts that are significantly more navigable and actionable for embodied agents according to reachability and interaction metrics. The method generalizes across different agent types by simply changing the agent abstraction, demonstrating that the same framework can tailor environments to service robots, warehouse robots, humans with varying capabilities, or even animals. The local refinement stage improves optimization efficiency and reduces failures due to local conflicts without sacrificing global layout quality.
AI.conclusion: RoboLayout successfully extends VLM-based layout generation to be agent-centric, not just visually and semantically grounded. By embedding explicit reachability constraints and using a two-stage optimization strategy with local refinement, it produces indoor layouts that are both semantically meaningful and practically functional for diverse embodied agents. This makes the framework more suitable for real-world applications such as robot deployment, assistive environment design, and simulation of agent-environment interactions, while retaining the strengths of the original LayoutVLM system.

title: Real-Time AI Service Economy: A Framework for Agentic Computing Across the Continuum
summary: Real-time AI services increasingly operate across the device-edge-cloud continuum, where autonomous AI agents generate latency-sensitive workloads, orchestrate multi-stage processing pipelines, and compete for shared resources under policy and governance constraints. This article shows that the structure of service-dependency graphs, modelled as DAGs whose nodes represent compute stages and whose edges encode execution ordering, is a primary determinant of whether decentralised, price-based resource allocation can work reliably at scale. When dependency graphs are hierarchical (tree or series-parallel), prices converge to stable equilibria, optimal allocations can be computed efficiently, and under appropriate mechanism design (with quasilinear utilities and discrete slice items), agents have no incentive to misreport their valuations within each decision epoch. When dependencies are more complex, with cross-cutting ties between pipeline stages, prices oscillate, allocation quality degrades, and the system becomes difficult to manage. To bridge this gap, we propose a hybrid management architecture in which cross-domain integrators encapsulate complex sub-graphs into resource slices that present a simpler, well-structured interface to the rest of the market. A systematic ablation study across six experiments (1,620 runs, 10 seeds each) confirms that (i) dependency-graph topology is a first-order determinant of price stability and scalability,(ii) the hybrid architecture reduces price volatility by up to 70-75% without sacrificing throughput, (iii) governance constraints create quantifiable efficiency-compliance trade-offs that depend jointly on topology and load, and (iv) under truthful bidding the decentralised market matches a centralised value-optimal baseline, confirming that decentralised coordination can replicate centralised allocation quality.

AI.tldr: The paper studies how the topology of AI service dependency graphs affects the stability and efficiency of decentralised, price-based resource allocation across device-edge-cloud systems, and proposes a hybrid architecture that encapsulates complex subgraphs to restore stability and performance.
AI.motivation: Real-time AI workloads increasingly span devices, edge nodes, and cloud data centers, forming multi-stage processing pipelines that share limited resources and must respect policies and governance constraints. Decentralised, market-based resource allocation is attractive for scalability and autonomy, but it is unclear under what conditions such markets remain stable, efficient, and incentive-compatible when services depend on each other via complex DAG-structured workflows. The authors aim to understand how dependency-graph structure impacts market dynamics and to design an architecture that enables stable decentralised coordination even with complex interdependencies and governance rules.
AI.method: The authors model AI service pipelines as directed acyclic graphs (DAGs) where nodes are compute stages and edges represent execution ordering constraints. They analyze different graph topologies (hierarchical vs. complex cross-cutting dependencies) in a decentralised price-based resource allocation framework with quasilinear utilities and discrete resource slices. They investigate equilibrium properties, price convergence, and incentive compatibility for hierarchical graphs and explore the failure modes (price oscillations, degraded allocations) that arise with more complex topologies. To mitigate these issues, they design a hybrid architecture that introduces cross-domain integrators which encapsulate complex subgraphs into higher-level resource slices with simpler, well-structured interfaces. They perform a systematic ablation study across six experimental setups (1,620 runs with 10 random seeds each) to evaluate price stability, throughput, and efficiency-compliance trade-offs under different topologies, loads, and governance constraints, comparing decentralised market outcomes with a centralised value-optimal baseline.
AI.result: The study shows that when service dependency graphs are hierarchical (tree or series-parallel), decentralised price-based mechanisms achieve stable price convergence, allow efficient computation of optimal allocations, and, assuming quasilinear utilities and discrete slices, ensure that agents are incentivised to report valuations truthfully within each decision epoch. In contrast, more complex graphs with cross-cutting dependencies cause price oscillations, lower allocation quality, and management difficulties. The proposed hybrid architecture, which encapsulates complex subgraphs behind integrator-managed resource slices, significantly reduces price volatility—by up to 70–75%—while maintaining throughput comparable to the baseline. Experiments further reveal that governance constraints induce measurable trade-offs between efficiency and compliance that are jointly shaped by graph topology and system load. Under truthful bidding, the decentralised market’s allocation quality matches that of a centralised value-optimal solution, demonstrating that decentralised coordination can reproduce centralised performance when the structural conditions are appropriate.
AI.conclusion: Dependency-graph topology is a critical factor in determining whether decentralised, price-based resource allocation for AI services across device-edge-cloud systems can be stable, efficient, and incentive-compatible. Hierarchical (tree or series-parallel) dependencies support stable equilibria and truthful behaviour, while complex cross-cutting ties undermine price stability and allocation quality. By introducing a hybrid architecture with cross-domain integrators that encapsulate complex subgraphs into simpler resource slices, the system can substantially reduce price volatility without sacrificing throughput. Governance constraints introduce predictable efficiency-compliance trade-offs that depend on both topology and workload. Overall, the work concludes that with appropriate structuring of dependencies and market interfaces, decentralised coordination can match the allocation quality of an idealised centralised controller.

title: Reasoning Models Struggle to Control their Chains of Thought
summary: Chain-of-thought (CoT) monitoring is a promising tool for detecting misbehaviors and understanding the motivations of modern reasoning models. However, if models can control what they verbalize in their CoT, it could undermine CoT monitorability. To measure this undesirable capability -- CoT controllability -- we introduce the CoT-Control evaluation suite, which includes tasks that require models to solve problems while adhering to CoT instructions, e.g., reasoning about a genetics question without using the word 'chromosome'. We show that reasoning models possess significantly lower CoT controllability than output controllability; for instance, Claude Sonnet 4.5 can control its CoT only 2.7% of the time but 61.9% when controlling its final output. We also find that CoT controllability is higher for larger models and decreases with more RL training, test-time compute, and increased problem difficulty. CoT controllability failures extend even to situations in which models are given incentives (as opposed to direct requests) to evade CoT monitors, although models exhibit slightly higher controllability when they are told they are being monitored. Similarly, eliciting controllability by adversarially optimizing prompts does not meaningfully increase controllability. Our results leave us cautiously optimistic that CoT controllability is currently unlikely to be a failure mode of CoT monitorability. However, the mechanism behind low controllability is not well understood. Given its importance for maintaining CoT monitorability, we recommend that frontier labs track CoT controllability in future models.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces CoT-Control, a benchmark to test whether language models can deliberately control their chain-of-thought (CoT) reasoning under constraints, and finds that current models largely fail to do so, which is good news for using CoT as a monitoring tool.
AI.motivation: Chain-of-thought (CoT) traces are used to monitor and understand model reasoning, detect misbehavior, and provide transparency. However, if models can strategically manipulate or hide what they write in their CoT while still solving tasks, then CoT-based monitoring could be bypassed. The authors are motivated to quantify this risk by measuring how much control models actually have over their internal verbalized reasoning, especially under instructions or incentives to avoid certain words or concepts.
AI.method: The authors propose the CoT-Control evaluation suite, a collection of tasks where models must both solve a problem and satisfy explicit constraints on their chain-of-thought text (e.g., answering a genetics question without using the word “chromosome” in their reasoning). They evaluate various reasoning models on two related dimensions: (1) CoT controllability—how often they can follow these constraints in their reasoning steps—and (2) output controllability—how often they can follow analogous constraints in their final answer only. They systematically vary model size, amount of RL training, test-time compute, and problem difficulty, and also test conditions where models are either directly instructed or indirectly incentivized to evade CoT monitors, as well as adversarially optimized prompts designed to elicit higher controllability.
AI.result: The experiments show that models have much lower controllability over their chain-of-thought than over their final answers. For example, Claude Sonnet 4.5 manages to satisfy CoT constraints only 2.7% of the time, compared to 61.9% success when constraints are applied to the final output. Larger models exhibit somewhat higher CoT controllability. In contrast, more RL training, more test-time compute, and harder tasks tend to reduce CoT controllability. Even when models are given incentives to evade CoT monitors, their ability to strategically control their CoT remains limited, though they do show slightly increased controllability when told they are being monitored. Adversarial prompt optimization does not substantially raise CoT controllability either.
AI.conclusion: The authors conclude that, at present, language models generally lack fine-grained control over their chain-of-thought, which reduces the likelihood that they can reliably conceal or strategically manipulate their reasoning to evade CoT-based monitoring. This is tentatively encouraging for the use of CoT as a safety and transparency tool. However, the underlying mechanisms causing low CoT controllability are not well understood, and could change as models advance. Consequently, the authors recommend that frontier labs systematically measure and track CoT controllability in new models to ensure CoT monitoring remains robust over time.

title: Evolving Medical Imaging Agents via Experience-driven Self-skill Discovery
summary: Clinical image interpretation is inherently multi-step and tool-centric: clinicians iteratively combine visual evidence with patient context, quantify findings, and refine their decisions through a sequence of specialized procedures. While LLM-based agents promise to orchestrate such heterogeneous medical tools, existing systems treat tool sets and invocation strategies as static after deployment. This design is brittle under real-world domain shifts, across tasks, and evolving diagnostic requirements, where predefined tool chains frequently degrade and demand costly manual re-design. We propose MACRO, a self-evolving, experience-augmented medical agent that shifts from static tool composition to experience-driven tool discovery. From verified execution trajectories, the agent autonomously identifies recurring effective multi-step tool sequences, synthesizes them into reusable composite tools, and registers these as new high-level primitives that continuously expand its behavioral repertoire. A lightweight image-feature memory grounds tool selection in a visual-clinical context, while a GRPO-like training loop reinforces reliable invocation of discovered composites, enabling closed-loop self-improvement with minimal supervision. Extensive experiments across diverse medical imaging datasets and tasks demonstrate that autonomous composite tool discovery consistently improves multi-step orchestration accuracy and cross-domain generalization over strong baselines and recent state-of-the-art agentic methods, bridging the gap between brittle static tool use and adaptive, context-aware clinical AI assistance. Code will be available upon acceptance.

AI.tldr: Proposes MACRO, a medical imaging agent that autonomously discovers and reuses composite tool chains from past successful trajectories, improving robustness and generalization of multi-step clinical image interpretation.
AI.motivation: Clinical image interpretation requires multi-step, tool-based workflows that integrate images, patient context, and quantification. Existing LLM-based agents use fixed tool sets and static invocation strategies, which break under domain shift, new tasks, or changing diagnostic needs, and need costly manual redesign. There is a need for agents that can adapt their tool compositions over time from experience.
AI.method: Introduce MACRO, a self-evolving medical agent. It logs verified execution trajectories, mines them to identify recurring effective multi-step tool sequences, and synthesizes these into reusable composite tools that become new high-level primitives. A lightweight image-feature memory grounds decisions in visual-clinical context. A GRPO-like reinforcement learning loop trains the agent to reliably choose and invoke these composites, forming a closed-loop self-improvement process with minimal supervision.
AI.result: Across multiple medical imaging datasets and tasks, MACRO’s autonomous composite tool discovery yields higher multi-step orchestration accuracy and better cross-domain generalization compared to strong baselines and recent agentic methods, demonstrating more robust tool use under varying conditions.
AI.conclusion: Experience-driven discovery and reuse of composite tools enables medical agents to move beyond brittle, static tool chains toward adaptive, context-aware clinical decision support. MACRO shows that self-evolving tool repertoires grounded in image and clinical context can significantly improve performance and robustness in medical image interpretation tasks.

title: Verify as You Go: An LLM-Powered Browser Extension for Fake News Detection
summary: The rampant spread of fake news in the digital age poses serious risks to public trust and democratic institutions, underscoring the need for effective, transparent, and user-centered detection tools. Existing browser extensions often fall short due to opaque model behavior, limited explanatory support, and a lack of meaningful user engagement. This paper introduces Aletheia, a novel browser extension that leverages Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) and Large Language Models (LLMs) to detect fake news and provide evidence-based explanations. Aletheia further includes two interactive components: a Discussion Hub that enables user dialogue around flagged content and a Stay Informed feature that surfaces recent fact-checks. Through extensive experiments, we show that Aletheia outperforms state-of-the-art baselines in detection performance. Complementing this empirical evaluation, a complementary user study with 250 participants confirms the system's usability and perceived effectiveness, highlighting its potential as a transparent tool for combating online fake news.

AI.tldr: Aletheia is a browser extension that uses RAG-enhanced LLMs plus interactive features to detect fake news, explain its decisions, and support user engagement, outperforming existing tools and being perceived as usable and effective.
AI.motivation: Fake news online threatens public trust and democratic processes, yet current detection extensions are opaque, offer weak explanations, and do not actively engage users. There is a need for a transparent, explainable, and user-centered tool that not only detects fake news but also supports understanding and discussion.
AI.method: The paper designs and implements Aletheia, a browser extension that combines Retrieval-Augmented Generation with Large Language Models to analyze online content, retrieve supporting evidence (e.g., fact-checks or reliable sources), and generate explanations. It adds two user-facing modules: (1) a Discussion Hub for user conversation on flagged items and (2) a Stay Informed panel that surfaces recent fact-checking information. The system is evaluated via quantitative experiments against state-of-the-art fake-news detectors and through a user study with 250 participants to assess usability and perceived effectiveness.
AI.result: Experimental results indicate that Aletheia achieves higher fake-news detection performance than state-of-the-art baseline systems. The 250-participant user study finds that users regard Aletheia as usable and effective, and they value its transparency and explanatory features.
AI.conclusion: Aletheia demonstrates that integrating RAG-powered LLMs with interactive, explanation-rich interfaces can improve both the technical performance and user acceptance of fake-news detection tools. The system appears to be a promising, transparent approach for helping users identify and understand questionable online content, suggesting future work on broader deployment and further refinement of user interaction mechanisms.

title: The World Won't Stay Still: Programmable Evolution for Agent Benchmarks
summary: LLM-powered agents fulfill user requests by interacting with environments, querying data, and invoking tools in a multi-turn process. Yet, most existing benchmarks assume static environments with fixed schemas and toolsets, neglecting the evolutionary nature of the real world and agents' robustness to environmental changes. In this paper, we study a crucial problem: how to evolve the agent environment in a scalable and controllable way, thereby better evaluating agents' adaptability to real-world dynamics. We propose ProEvolve, a graph-based framework that makes environment evolution programmable. At its core, a typed relational graph provides a unified, explicit representation of the environment: data, tools, and schema. Under this formalism, adding, removing, or modifying capabilities are expressed as graph transformations that coherently propagate updates across tools, schemas, and data access. Building on this, ProEvolve can (1) program the evolutionary dynamics as graph transformations to generate environments automatically, and (2) instantiate task sandboxes via subgraph sampling and programming. We validate ProEvolve by evolving a single environment into 200 environments and 3,000 task sandboxes, and benchmark representative agents accordingly.

AI.tldr: ProEvolve is a graph-based framework for programmatically evolving LLM-agent environments to test robustness to changing tools, data, and schemas.
AI.motivation: Existing LLM-agent benchmarks assume static environments with fixed schemas and toolsets, which fails to capture real-world dynamics where tools, data sources, and interfaces change over time. There is a need for a scalable, controllable way to evolve environments so we can systematically evaluate how well agents adapt to such changes.
AI.method: The authors introduce ProEvolve, which represents the entire agent environment—data, tools, and schemas—as a typed relational graph. Environment evolution operations (adding/removing/modifying tools, data, or schemas) are formalized as graph transformations that consistently propagate changes. Using these primitives, ProEvolve (1) automatically generates evolved environments by programming evolutionary dynamics as graph transformations, and (2) creates specific task sandboxes via subgraph sampling and additional programming.
AI.result: Using ProEvolve, the authors evolve a single base environment into 200 distinct evolved environments and 3,000 task sandboxes. They then benchmark various representative LLM-based agents within these diverse and evolving environments to evaluate performance and robustness, though detailed metrics are not provided in the abstract.
AI.conclusion: A graph-based, programmable evolution framework like ProEvolve enables systematic, scalable creation of dynamic environments and task sandboxes, providing a more realistic and fine-grained way to evaluate LLM agents’ adaptability to real-world environmental changes than traditional static benchmarks.

title: DeepFact: Co-Evolving Benchmarks and Agents for Deep Research Factuality
summary: Search-augmented LLM agents can produce deep research reports (DRRs), but verifying claim-level factuality remains challenging. Existing fact-checkers are primarily designed for general-domain, factoid-style atomic claims, and there is no benchmark to test whether such verifiers transfer to DRRs. Yet building such a benchmark is itself difficult. We first show that static expert-labeled benchmarks are brittle in this setting: in a controlled study with PhD-level specialists, unassisted experts achieve only 60.8% accuracy on a hidden micro-gold set of verifiable claims. We propose Evolving Benchmarking via Audit-then-Score (AtS), where benchmark labels and rationales are explicitly revisable: when a verifier disagrees with the current benchmark, it must submit evidence; an auditor adjudicates the dispute; and accepted revisions update the benchmark before models are scored. Across four AtS rounds, expert micro-gold accuracy rises to 90.9%, indicating experts are substantially more reliable as auditors than as one-shot labelers. We instantiate AtS as DeepFact-Bench, a versioned DRR factuality benchmark with auditable rationales, and DeepFact-Eval, a document-level verification agent (with a grouped lite variant) that outperforms existing verifiers on DeepFact-Bench and transfers well to external factuality datasets.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces an evolving benchmark and evaluation method for checking factual accuracy in long, research-style reports generated by LLM agents, showing that iterative audit-and-revision yields much more reliable expert labels than one-shot annotation and enabling better factuality verifiers.
AI.motivation: Search-augmented LLMs can create complex, deep research reports, but verifying each claim’s factual correctness is hard. Existing fact-checking benchmarks and models focus on short, simple, factoid-like claims and may not transfer to this more complex setting. Building a robust benchmark for such detailed documents is itself difficult because even domain experts are surprisingly unreliable when asked to label claims in one pass, which undermines trust in static, expert-labeled datasets.
AI.method: The authors first run a controlled study with PhD-level specialists evaluating verifiable claims in DRRs and measure their accuracy on a hidden micro-gold subset, demonstrating brittleness of static one-shot labels. They then propose Audit-then-Score (AtS), an evolving benchmarking protocol: when an automatic verifier disagrees with an existing benchmark label, it must provide evidence; a human auditor reviews the dispute and decides whether to revise the label and rationale. Accepted revisions update the benchmark, and this process is repeated in multiple rounds. They instantiate this protocol in two artifacts: DeepFact-Bench, a versioned factuality benchmark for DRRs with explicit, auditable rationales, and DeepFact-Eval, a document-level verification agent (plus a grouped lite variant) that uses the benchmark and the AtS process for training and assessment.
AI.result: In the initial expert study, unassisted PhD-level experts achieve only 60.8% accuracy on a hidden micro-gold set of verifiable claims, showing that static benchmarks based on one-shot expert labels are unreliable. After four rounds of the AtS process, expert micro-gold accuracy on the benchmark increases to 90.9%, indicating that experts function much more reliably as auditors of disputes than as initial labelers. DeepFact-Eval, the proposed verification agent, outperforms existing factuality verifiers on DeepFact-Bench and also shows good transfer performance to other external factuality datasets, validating the effectiveness of both the benchmark and the evaluation protocol.
AI.conclusion: Static, expert-labeled factuality benchmarks for complex, research-style LLM outputs are brittle and unreliable, even with highly trained annotators. An evolving audit-based benchmarking protocol, Audit-then-Score, where verifiers must contest labels with evidence and human auditors adjudicate, can substantially improve label quality over time. The resulting DeepFact-Bench and DeepFact-Eval provide a more dependable framework and tools for measuring and improving claim-level factual accuracy in deep research reports and show strong empirical performance, suggesting that iterative, evidence-driven auditing is a promising paradigm for factuality evaluation in complex LLM-generated content.

title: NOTAI.AI: Explainable Detection of Machine-Generated Text via Curvature and Feature Attribution
summary: We present NOTAI.AI, an explainable framework for machine-generated text detection that extends Fast-DetectGPT by integrating curvature-based signals with neural and stylometric features in a supervised setting. The system combines 17 interpretable features, including Conditional Probability Curvature, ModernBERT detector score, readability metrics, and stylometric cues, within a gradient-boosted tree (XGBoost) meta-classifier to determine whether a text is human- or AI-generated. Furthermore, NOTAI.AI applies Shapley Additive Explanations (SHAP) to provide both local and global feature-level attribution. These attributions are further translated into structured natural-language rationales through an LLM-based explanation layer, which enables user-facing interpretability. The system is deployed as an interactive web application that supports real-time analysis, visual feature inspection, and structured evidence presentation. A web interface allows users to input text and inspect how neural and statistical signals influence the final decision. The source code and demo video are publicly available to support reproducibility.

AI.tldr: NOTAI.AI is an explainable AI-text detector that combines curvature-based, neural, and stylometric features in an XGBoost model and explains its decisions with SHAP-based, LLM-generated natural-language rationales.
AI.motivation: Existing AI text detectors like Fast-DetectGPT either rely on limited signal types (e.g., curvature-based features only) or provide little interpretability for end users. There is a growing need for reliable, user-understandable tools that can distinguish human from machine-generated text, particularly as LLM outputs become more human-like and are used in sensitive domains.
AI.method: The authors extend Fast-DetectGPT by integrating Conditional Probability Curvature with additional neural (e.g., ModernBERT detector scores), readability, and stylometric metrics to form a set of 17 interpretable features. These features are fed into a supervised gradient-boosted tree (XGBoost) meta-classifier that outputs a human vs AI decision. For interpretability, they compute SHAP values to obtain local and global feature attributions and then use a large language model to translate these attributions into structured natural-language rationales. The whole system is wrapped in an interactive web interface that visualizes features, attributions, and final decisions in real time.
AI.result: The framework successfully combines multiple complementary signals into a unified supervised classifier and produces human-readable explanations at both feature and decision levels. It is implemented as an interactive web application with real-time analysis and visual inspection capabilities, and its source code and demonstration materials are publicly released, facilitating reproducibility and practical use.
AI.conclusion: NOTAI.AI demonstrates that integrating curvature-based, neural, and stylometric features within an explainable supervised framework can improve both the functionality and interpretability of AI-text detection. The SHAP- and LLM-based explanation layer makes detector decisions more transparent and user-friendly, and the public release of code and demo positions the system as a reproducible, practical tool for detecting machine-generated text.

title: An Interactive Multi-Agent System for Evaluation of New Product Concepts
comment: 46 pages, 3 figures + This paper proposes an LLM-based multi-agent system (MAS) for automated evaluation of new product concepts, incorporating retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) and cross-functional virtual agents to assess technical and market feasibility
summary: Product concept evaluation is a critical stage that determines strategic resource allocation and project success in enterprises. However, traditional expert-led approaches face limitations such as subjective bias and high time and cost requirements. To support this process, this study proposes an automated approach utilizing a large language model (LLM)-based multi-agent system (MAS). Through a systematic analysis of previous research on product development and team collaboration, this study established two primary evaluation dimensions, namely technical feasibility and market feasibility. The proposed system consists of a team of eight virtual agents representing specialized domains such as R&D and marketing. These agents use retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) and real-time search tools to gather objective evidence and validate concepts through structured deliberations based on the established criteria. The agents were further fine-tuned using professional product review data to enhance their judgment accuracy. A case study involving professional display monitor concepts demonstrated that the system's evaluation rankings were consistent with those of senior industry experts. These results confirm the usability of the proposed multi-agent-based evaluation approach for supporting product development decisions.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes an automated, LLM-based multi-agent system to evaluate product concepts along technical and market feasibility, achieving rankings consistent with senior experts.
AI.motivation: Product concept evaluation heavily affects resource allocation and project success, but current expert-based methods suffer from subjectivity, high time and financial costs, and limited scalability. There is a need for a more objective, efficient, and repeatable evaluation approach that can still reflect domain expertise.
AI.method: The authors design a large language model-based multi-agent system comprising eight virtual agents, each representing a specialized function such as R&D or marketing. They define two main evaluation dimensions: technical feasibility and market feasibility, derived from prior research on product development and team collaboration. The agents employ retrieval-augmented generation and real-time search tools to collect up-to-date, objective information, then engage in structured deliberation based on predefined criteria. The LLM agents are further fine-tuned on professional product review data to improve the accuracy and consistency of their judgments. The system is tested via a case study on professional display monitor concepts.
AI.result: In the case study, the evaluation rankings produced by the multi-agent system align closely with those of senior industry experts, indicating that the system can approximate expert-level judgment. This demonstrates that the approach is both usable and reliable for practical product concept evaluation tasks.
AI.conclusion: An LLM-based multi-agent system, grounded in well-defined technical and market feasibility dimensions and enhanced with RAG and domain-specific fine-tuning, can effectively support product concept evaluation. The system reduces reliance on purely human expert evaluation, potentially lowering time and cost while maintaining expert-level decision quality, and offers a viable tool for aiding product development decisions.

title: Safer Reasoning Traces: Measuring and Mitigating Chain-of-Thought Leakage in LLMs
summary: Chain-of-Thought (CoT) prompting improves LLM reasoning but can increase privacy risk by resurfacing personally identifiable information (PII) from the prompt into reasoning traces and outputs, even under policies that instruct the model not to restate PII. We study such direct, inference-time PII leakage using a model-agnostic framework that (i) defines leakage as risk-weighted, token-level events across 11 PII types, (ii) traces leakage curves as a function of the allowed CoT budget, and (iii) compares open- and closed-source model families on a structured PII dataset with a hierarchical risk taxonomy. We find that CoT consistently elevates leakage, especially for high-risk categories, and that leakage is strongly family- and budget-dependent. Increasing the reasoning budget can either amplify or attenuate leakage depending on the base model. We then benchmark lightweight inference-time gatekeepers: a rule-based detector, a TF-IDF + logistic regression classifier, a GLiNER-based NER model, and an LLM-as-judge, using risk-weighted F1, Macro-F1, and recall. No single method dominates across models or budgets, motivating hybrid, style-adaptive gatekeeping policies that balance utility and risk under a common, reproducible protocol.

AI.tldr: The paper analyzes how Chain-of-Thought prompting in LLMs increases the risk of leaking personally identifiable information and evaluates lightweight gatekeeping methods to mitigate this leakage under a unified, risk-aware framework.
AI.motivation: While Chain-of-Thought prompting improves reasoning performance in large language models, it may inadvertently resurface and expose personally identifiable information present in prompts or contexts, even when models are instructed not to do so. There is a lack of systematic, model-agnostic measurement of such inference-time PII leakage, especially across different PII types, risk levels, model families, and reasoning budgets. Moreover, practitioners need practical, low-overhead gatekeeping mechanisms that can reduce leakage without heavily compromising model utility.
AI.method: The authors construct a model-agnostic evaluation framework for inference-time PII leakage that: (i) defines leakage at the token level with risk-weighted events across 11 types of PII, organized by a hierarchical risk taxonomy; (ii) measures leakage as a function of the allowed Chain-of-Thought (CoT) token budget, producing leakage curves; and (iii) compares multiple open- and closed-source LLM families on a structured PII dataset. They also benchmark several lightweight gatekeeping approaches at inference time: a simple rule-based detector, a TF-IDF plus logistic regression text classifier, a GLiNER-based named entity recognition model, and an LLM used as a judge. These gatekeepers are evaluated with risk-weighted F1, Macro-F1, and recall metrics.
AI.result: The study finds that enabling Chain-of-Thought prompting systematically increases PII leakage across models, particularly for higher-risk PII categories. Leakage magnitude depends strongly on the model family and on the size of the CoT token budget: for some base models, increasing the reasoning budget amplifies leakage, while for others it can attenuate it. In benchmarking gatekeepers, none of the evaluated methods (rule-based, TF-IDF+LR, GLiNER NER, LLM-as-judge) consistently outperforms the others across all models and CoT budgets. Performance varies by setting and metric (risk-weighted F1, Macro-F1, recall), highlighting trade-offs between strict protection and preserving useful reasoning content.
AI.conclusion: Chain-of-Thought prompting poses a measurable and sometimes substantial privacy risk by increasing inference-time PII leakage, which is sensitive to both model family and reasoning budget. Because no single lightweight gatekeeping strategy is uniformly best, effective mitigation will likely require hybrid, style-adaptive gatekeeping policies that can flexibly manage the trade-off between privacy risk and task utility. The proposed risk-aware, model-agnostic protocol provides a reproducible basis for future work on privacy-preserving reasoning with LLMs.

title: Agentic LLM Planning via Step-Wise PDDL Simulation: An Empirical Characterisation
summary: Task planning, the problem of sequencing actions to reach a goal from an initial state, is a core capability requirement for autonomous robotic systems. Whether large language models (LLMs) can serve as viable planners alongside classical symbolic methods remains an open question. We present PyPDDLEngine, an open-source Planning Domain Definition Language (PDDL) simulation engine that exposes planning operations as LLM tool calls through a Model Context Protocol (MCP) interface. Rather than committing to a complete action sequence upfront, the LLM acts as an interactive search policy that selects one action at a time, observes each resulting state, and can reset and retry. We evaluate four approaches on 102 International Planning Competition (IPC) Blocksworld instances under a uniform 180-second budget: Fast Downward lama-first and seq-sat-lama-2011 as classical baselines, direct LLM planning (Claude Haiku 4.5), and agentic LLM planning via PyPDDLEngine. Fast Downward achieves 85.3% success. The direct and agentic LLM approaches achieve 63.7% and 66.7%, respectively, a consistent but modest three-percentage-point advantage for the agentic approach at $5.7\times$ higher token cost per solution. Across most co-solved difficulty blocks, both LLM approaches produce shorter plans than seq-sat-lama-2011 despite its iterative quality improvement, a result consistent with training-data recall rather than generalisable planning. These results suggest that agentic gains depend on the nature of environmental feedback. Coding agents benefit from externally grounded signals such as compiler errors and test failures, whereas PDDL step feedback is self-assessed, leaving the agent to evaluate its own progress without external verification.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces PyPDDLEngine, an MCP-exposed PDDL simulation engine that lets LLMs plan interactively one step at a time, and empirically compares classical planners, direct LLM planning, and agentic LLM planning on Blocksworld tasks.
AI.motivation: To investigate whether large language models can serve as effective task planners compared to classical symbolic planners, and to understand when agentic, tool-using LLM setups actually yield planning benefits.
AI.method: They build PyPDDLEngine, a PDDL simulation engine exposed as an MCP tool so an LLM can choose one action at a time, observe resulting states, and reset/retry. They then run a controlled evaluation on 102 IPC Blocksworld instances under a fixed 180-second budget, comparing two Fast Downward configurations with (1) direct LLM planning and (2) agentic LLM planning via PyPDDLEngine, measuring success rates, token costs, and plan lengths across difficulty levels.
AI.result: Fast Downward with lama-first reaches 85.3% success. Direct LLM planning (Claude Haiku 4.5) solves 63.7% of tasks; the agentic LLM using PyPDDLEngine solves 66.7%, showing a consistent but small ~3% improvement at a 5.7x higher token cost per solution. In many shared instances, both LLM approaches generate shorter plans than the seq-sat-lama-2011 configuration, suggesting memorised patterns rather than true search-based planning.
AI.conclusion: LLMs still lag behind state-of-the-art classical planners on symbolic planning benchmarks, and making them agentic with a PDDL simulation tool yields only modest gains at substantial extra cost. The findings indicate that agentic improvements depend heavily on externally verifiable feedback; unlike coding agents that get compiler/test signals, PDDL agents largely self-assess their progress, limiting the effectiveness of interactive tool use for planning.

title: The Fragility Of Moral Judgment In Large Language Models
summary:
People increasingly use large language models (LLMs) for everyday moral and interpersonal guidance, yet these systems cannot interrogate missing context and judge dilemmas as presented. We introduce a perturbation framework for testing the stability and manipulability of LLM moral judgments while holding the underlying moral conflict constant. Using 2,939 dilemmas from r/AmItheAsshole (January-March 2025), we generate three families of content perturbations: surface edits (lexical/structural noise), point-of-view shifts (voice and stance neutralization), and persuasion cues (self-positioning, social proof, pattern admissions, victim framing). We also vary the evaluation protocol (output ordering, instruction placement, and unstructured prompting). We evaluated all variants with four models (GPT-4.1, Claude 3.7 Sonnet, DeepSeek V3, Qwen2.5-72B) (N=129,156 judgments).
  Surface perturbations produce low flip rates (7.5%), largely within the self-consistency noise floor (4-13%), whereas point-of-view shifts induce substantially higher instability (24.3%). A large subset of dilemmas (37.9%) is robust to surface noise yet flips under perspective changes, indicating that models condition on narrative voice as a pragmatic cue. Instability concentrates in morally ambiguous cases; scenarios where no party is assigned blame are most susceptible. Persuasion perturbations yield systematic directional shifts. Protocol choices dominate all other factors: agreement between structured protocols is only 67.6% (kappa=0.55), and only 35.7% of model-scenario units match across all three protocols. These results show that LLM moral judgments are co-produced by narrative form and task scaffolding, raising reproducibility and equity concerns when outcomes depend on presentation skill rather than moral substance.

AI.tldr: The paper tests how stable large language models’ moral judgments are when the same underlying dilemma is phrased or framed differently, finding that judgments shift substantially with perspective, persuasive framing, and evaluation protocol.
AI.motivation: As people increasingly rely on LLMs for everyday moral and interpersonal advice, it is crucial to know whether these systems give consistent judgments based on moral content or whether their outputs can be easily manipulated by wording, narrative perspective, or prompt format. Inconsistent or manipulable judgments raise concerns for fairness, reproducibility, and trustworthiness of LLM-based guidance.
AI.method: The authors build a perturbation framework that systematically alters the presentation of real-world moral dilemmas while keeping the core moral conflict constant. They scrape 2,939 dilemmas from the subreddit r/AmItheAsshole (January–March 2025) and generate three classes of controlled perturbations: (1) surface edits such as lexical and structural noise; (2) point-of-view shifts involving changes in narrative voice and neutralizing the stance of the narrator; and (3) persuasion cues such as self-positioning, social proof, pattern admissions, and victim framing. Additionally, they vary evaluation protocols, including output ordering, where instructions appear in the prompt, and whether the prompt is structured or unstructured. They then query four LLMs (GPT‑4.1, Claude 3.7 Sonnet, DeepSeek V3, Qwen2.5‑72B) on all variants, yielding 129,156 total moral judgments. They compute flip rates (how often the model’s judgment changes across perturbations) and agreement measures (e.g., Cohen’s kappa) to quantify instability and protocol sensitivity.
AI.result: Surface-level perturbations cause relatively low instability in moral judgments, with flip rates around 7.5%, which is close to the models’ inherent self-consistency noise floor (4–13%). In contrast, point-of-view perturbations create much higher instability, with a 24.3% flip rate. A notable portion of dilemmas (37.9%) remains stable under surface noise but flips with perspective shifts, indicating strong sensitivity to narrative voice as a pragmatic signal. Instability is especially high for morally ambiguous dilemmas and cases where the model assigns no blame to any party. Persuasive framing perturbations lead to systematic, directional changes in judgments rather than mere noise. Across all factors, evaluation protocol choices have the largest impact: structured protocols agree with each other only 67.6% of the time (kappa = 0.55), and just 35.7% of model–scenario combinations yield matching outputs across all three tested protocols.
AI.conclusion: The study concludes that LLM moral judgments are not determined solely by the underlying moral facts of a scenario; instead, they are co-produced by the narrative form of the dilemma and the scaffolding of the evaluation prompt. Because minor shifts in perspective, persuasive cues, and prompting protocol can substantially change the system’s moral verdicts—especially in ambiguous cases—reproducibility and equity are at risk. In practice, people’s ability to phrase and frame their dilemmas can differentially influence outcomes, meaning that moral guidance from LLMs may reward presentation skill rather than reflect stable moral reasoning over the same situation.

title: Aggregative Semantics for Quantitative Bipolar Argumentation Frameworks
summary: Formal argumentation is being used increasingly in artificial intelligence as an effective and understandable way to model potentially conflicting pieces of information, called arguments, and identify so-called acceptable arguments depending on a chosen semantics. This paper deals with the specific context of Quantitative Bipolar Argumentation Frameworks (QBAF), where arguments have intrinsic weights and can attack or support each other. In this context, we introduce a novel family of gradual semantics, called aggregative semantics. In order to deal with situations in which attackers and supporters do not play a symmetric role, and in contrast to modular semantics, we propose to aggregate attackers and supporters separately. This leads to a three-stage computation, which consists in computing a global weight for attackers and another for supporters, before aggregating these two values with the intrinsic weight of the argument. We discuss the properties that the three aggregation functions should satisfy depending on the context, as well as their relationships with the classical principles for gradual semantics. This discussion is supported by various simple examples, as well as a final example on which five hundred aggregative semantics are tested and compared, illustrating the range of possible behaviours with aggregative semantics. Decomposing the computation into three distinct and interpretable steps leads to a more parametrisable computation: it keeps the bipolarity one step further than what is done in the literature, and it leads to more understandable gradual semantics.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a new family of semantics, called aggregative semantics, for Quantitative Bipolar Argumentation Frameworks, which compute argument strengths in three interpretable aggregation steps.
AI.motivation: Existing gradual semantics for Quantitative Bipolar Argumentation Frameworks often treat attackers and supporters in a symmetric or modular way, which may not accurately reflect situations where attacks and supports play different roles. There is a need for more flexible, parameterisable, and interpretable ways of computing the acceptability degree of arguments that preserve bipolarity further into the computation.
AI.method: The authors define a new family of gradual semantics, termed aggregative semantics, for QBAFs. They design a three-stage computation: (1) aggregate all attackers of an argument into a single global attacker weight using an attacker aggregation function; (2) aggregate all supporters into a single global supporter weight using a supporter aggregation function; (3) combine these two global values with the argument’s intrinsic weight via a third aggregation function to obtain the final strength of the argument. They then formalise and analyse properties that each of the three aggregation functions should satisfy under different contexts and compare these with established principles for gradual semantics. Their analysis is illustrated by toy examples and a more substantial case study where about five hundred different instantiations of aggregative semantics are tested and compared.
AI.result: The paper provides a formal characterisation of aggregative semantics and identifies constraints and desirable properties for the three aggregation functions involved. Through examples and a large-scale comparison of roughly five hundred concrete semantics, they show that the framework can exhibit a wide variety of behaviours, demonstrating its expressiveness and flexibility. The three-step structure preserves the explicit handling of attacks and supports longer than previous approaches and yields semantics that are easier to interpret.
AI.conclusion: Aggregative semantics offer a more flexible and interpretable way to evaluate arguments in Quantitative Bipolar Argumentation Frameworks by separately aggregating attackers and supporters and then combining them with intrinsic argument weights. This three-stage, parametrised design keeps the bipolar structure explicit, aligns with or clarifies classical principles for gradual semantics, and allows many different but systematically comparable semantics to be defined within a single unifying framework.

title: FreeTxt-Vi: A Benchmarked Vietnamese-English Toolkit for Segmentation, Sentiment, and Summarisation
summary: FreeTxt-Vi is a free and open source web based toolkit for creating and analysing bilingual Vietnamese English text collections. Positioned at the intersection of corpus linguistics and natural language processing NLP it enables users to build explore and interpret free text data without requiring programming expertise. The system combines corpus analysis features such as concordancing keyword analysis word relation exploration and interactive visualisation with transformer based NLP components for sentiment analysis and summarisation. A key contribution of this work is the design of a unified bilingual NLP pipeline that integrates a hybrid VnCoreNLP and Byte Pair Encoding BPE segmentation strategy a fine tuned TabularisAI sentiment classifier and a fine tuned Qwen2.5 model for abstractive summarisation. Unlike existing text analysis platforms FreeTxt Vi is evaluated as a set of language processing components. We conduct a three part evaluation covering segmentation sentiment analysis and summarisation and show that our approach achieves competitive or superior performance compared to widely used baselines in both Vietnamese and English. By reducing technical barriers to multilingual text analysis FreeTxt Vi supports reproducible research and promotes the development of language resources for Vietnamese a widely spoken but underrepresented language in NLP. The toolkit is applicable to domains including education digital humanities cultural heritage and the social sciences where qualitative text data are common but often difficult to process at scale.

AI.tldr: A web-based open-source toolkit, FreeTxt-Vi, enables non-programmers to build and analyze bilingual Vietnamese–English text corpora with integrated corpus-linguistic tools and transformer-based NLP components, achieving competitive performance in segmentation, sentiment analysis, and summarization for both languages.
AI.motivation: There is a lack of easy-to-use, open, and well-evaluated tools for analyzing Vietnamese–English free text, especially for non-programmers. Vietnamese is underrepresented in NLP despite being widely spoken, and existing platforms rarely provide unified bilingual pipelines or component-level evaluation. Researchers and practitioners in areas like education, digital humanities, and social sciences need scalable ways to process qualitative text data without deep technical skills.
AI.method: The authors design and implement FreeTxt-Vi, a web-based toolkit that integrates traditional corpus linguistics functionalities (e.g., concordancing, keyword analysis, word relation exploration, interactive visualization) with modern transformer-based NLP modules. They build a unified bilingual processing pipeline using a hybrid segmentation approach that combines VnCoreNLP and Byte Pair Encoding (BPE). On top of this, they fine-tune a TabularisAI-based sentiment classifier and a Qwen2.5 model for abstractive summarization. They then conduct a three-part empirical evaluation, separately assessing segmentation, sentiment analysis, and summarization performance against commonly used baselines in both Vietnamese and English.
AI.result: The unified bilingual NLP pipeline in FreeTxt-Vi attains competitive or better performance compared with popular baseline systems across three tasks: word segmentation, sentiment analysis, and abstractive summarization, in both Vietnamese and English. The evaluations demonstrate that their hybrid segmentation strategy and fine-tuned transformer-based models are effective in a multilingual, practical toolkit setting.
AI.conclusion: FreeTxt-Vi successfully lowers the technical barrier for multilingual (Vietnamese–English) text analysis by combining corpus linguistics tools with transformer-based NLP in an accessible web interface. Its evaluated, component-based design supports reproducible research and contributes to strengthening NLP resources for Vietnamese, an underrepresented language. The toolkit is broadly applicable to text-centric domains such as education, digital humanities, cultural heritage, and social sciences, where large qualitative text collections need to be explored and interpreted at scale.

title: Offline Materials Optimization with CliqueFlowmer
summary: Recent advances in deep learning inspired neural network-based approaches to computational materials discovery (CMD). A plethora of problems in this field involve finding materials that optimize a target property. Nevertheless, the increasingly popular generative modeling methods are ineffective at boldly exploring attractive regions of the materials space due to their maximum likelihood training. In this work, we offer an alternative CMD technique based on offline model-based optimization (MBO) that fuses direct optimization of a target material property into generation. To that end, we introduce a domain-specific model, dubbed CliqueFlowmer, that incorporates recent advances of clique-based MBO into transformer and flow generation. We validate CliqueFlowmer's optimization abilities and show that materials it produces strongly outperform those provided by generative baselines. To enable employment of CliqueFlowmer in specialized materials optimization problems and support interdisciplinary research, we open-source our code at https://github.com/znowu/CliqueFlowmer.

AI.tldr: They propose CliqueFlowmer, a new offline model-based optimization method that directly optimizes material properties during generative modeling, outperforming standard generative approaches for computational materials discovery.
AI.motivation: Existing neural generative models for computational materials discovery are trained with maximum likelihood, which makes them conservative samplers that poorly explore high-performing but low-probability regions of materials space. There is a need for methods that directly target and optimize desired material properties while generating candidates.
AI.method: They develop CliqueFlowmer, a domain-specific model that combines clique-based offline model-based optimization with transformer and normalizing flow generative architectures. The model integrates property optimization into the generation process, using pre-collected data (offline setting) and clique-based strategies to more effectively search material space for high-performing candidates.
AI.result: CliqueFlowmer is empirically validated on materials optimization tasks and is shown to produce materials whose target properties are significantly better than those produced by baseline generative models trained via maximum likelihood.
AI.conclusion: Integrating clique-based offline model-based optimization with transformer and flow generators yields a powerful CMD tool that more effectively discovers high-performing materials than standard generative baselines. The open-sourced implementation facilitates its application to various specialized materials optimization problems and broader interdisciplinary use.

title: Towards Robust Retrieval-Augmented Generation Based on Knowledge Graph: A Comparative Analysis
comment: The paper is 6 pages long and includes 5 figures and 3 tables illustrating the experimental framework and results. It is submitted to the IEEE International Conference on Systems, Man, and Cybernetics (SMC 2025) and studies improving the robustness of Retrieval-Augmented Generation systems using knowledge graph based GraphRAG approaches
summary: Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) was introduced to enhance the capabilities of Large Language Models (LLMs) beyond their encoded prior knowledge. This is achieved by providing LLMs with an external source of knowledge, which helps reduce factual hallucinations and enables access to new information not available during pretraining. However, inconsistent retrieved information can negatively affect LLM responses. The Retrieval-Augmented Generation Benchmark (RGB) was introduced to evaluate the robustness of RAG systems under such conditions. In this work, we use the RGB corpus to evaluate LLMs in four scenarios: noise robustness, information integration, negative rejection, and counterfactual robustness. We perform a comparative analysis between the RGB RAG baseline and GraphRAG, a knowledge graph based retrieval system. We test three GraphRAG customizations to improve robustness. Results show improvements over the RGB baseline and provide insights for designing more reliable RAG systems for real world scenarios.

AI.tldr: The paper evaluates and improves the robustness of Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) systems, comparing a baseline to GraphRAG on the RGB benchmark across four challenging scenarios.
AI.motivation: RAG augments LLMs with external knowledge to reduce hallucinations and provide up-to-date information, but inconsistent or noisy retrieved data can harm answer quality. Existing benchmarks like RGB expose weaknesses in robustness, creating a need to understand and improve how different RAG architectures handle noisy, conflicting, missing, or misleading evidence.
AI.method: Using the RGB benchmark, the authors systematically test LLMs under four types of challenging retrieval conditions: noise robustness, information integration, negative rejection, and counterfactual robustness. They compare a standard RGB RAG baseline to GraphRAG, which uses a knowledge-graph-based retrieval mechanism. They further design and evaluate three custom variants (customizations) of GraphRAG aimed specifically at improving robustness in these scenarios.
AI.result: Across the RGB tasks, the customized GraphRAG settings outperform the RGB RAG baseline, demonstrating better handling of noisy, conflicting, or misleading retrieved information. The experiments highlight where graph-based retrieval helps the most and how different customizations affect robustness metrics.
AI.conclusion: Knowledge-graph-based retrieval (GraphRAG), especially when customized, can make RAG systems more robust to real-world retrieval issues such as noise, conflicting information, and counterfactuals. The study offers practical guidance for designing more reliable RAG pipelines and underscores RGB as a useful benchmark for stress-testing RAG robustness.

title: Conversational Demand Response: Bidirectional Aggregator-Prosumer Coordination through Agentic AI
comment: 6 pages, 2 figures. Code available at: https://github.com/RedaElMakroum/cdr
summary: Residential demand response depends on sustained prosumer participation, yet existing coordination is either fully automated, or limited to one-way dispatch signals and price alerts that offer little possibility for informed decision-making. This paper introduces Conversational Demand Response (CDR), a coordination mechanism where aggregators and prosumers interact through bidirectional natural language, enabled through agentic AI. A two-tier multi-agent architecture is developed in which an aggregator agent dispatches flexibility requests and a prosumer Home Energy Management System (HEMS) assesses deliverability and cost-benefit by calling an optimization-based tool. CDR also enables prosumer-initiated upstream communication, where changes in preferences can reach the aggregator directly. Proof-of-concept evaluation shows that interactions complete in under 12 seconds. The architecture illustrates how agentic AI can bridge the aggregator-prosumer coordination gap, providing the scalability of automated DR while preserving the transparency, explainability, and user agency necessary for sustained prosumer participation. All system components, including agent prompts, orchestration logic, and simulation interfaces, are released as open source to enable reproducibility and further development.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes Conversational Demand Response (CDR), where aggregators and residential prosumers coordinate via natural-language interactions powered by agentic AI, combining automation with user transparency and control.
AI.motivation: Residential demand response programs need ongoing participation from prosumers, but current systems are either fully automated black boxes or rely on one-way signals (e.g., price alerts) that do not support informed choices or nuanced feedback. This gap reduces user trust, transparency, and willingness to keep participating. The paper aims to design a coordination mechanism that scales like automated DR while maintaining user agency, explainability, and continuous engagement.
AI.method: The authors design a two-tier multi-agent architecture using agentic AI. An aggregator agent sends flexibility requests to prosumers. On the prosumer side, a Home Energy Management System (HEMS) agent receives the request and calls an optimization-based tool to evaluate feasibility and cost-benefit (e.g., whether and how much flexibility can be provided). The system supports bidirectional, natural-language dialogue: (1) aggregator-to-prosumer requests and clarifications; (2) prosumer-to-aggregator communication where users can change preferences or constraints, which are then translated into machine-readable parameters. They implement this architecture, including prompts, orchestration logic, and simulation interfaces, and conduct a proof-of-concept evaluation focused on interaction latency and behavior.
AI.result: The implemented CDR prototype handles complete interactions between aggregator and prosumer (via the HEMS agent) in under 12 seconds, demonstrating that natural-language, AI-mediated coordination can be responsive enough for practical DR scenarios. The system supports both top-down flexibility requests and bottom-up prosumer-initiated updates to preferences. All components (prompts, orchestration, and simulation tools) are made available as open source to support reproducibility and extension.
AI.conclusion: Conversational Demand Response, realized through an agentic AI multi-agent architecture, can bridge the gap between fully automated DR and user-centric participation. It offers the scalability and efficiency of automated coordination while preserving transparency, explainability, and user agency, which are crucial for sustained prosumer engagement. The open-source release invites further research and development, suggesting that conversational, AI-mediated coordination could become a practical paradigm for future residential demand response systems.

title: Cultural Perspectives and Expectations for Generative AI: A Global Survey Approach
summary: There is a lack of empirical evidence about global attitudes around whether and how GenAI should represent cultures. This paper assesses understandings and beliefs about culture as it relates to GenAI from a large-scale global survey. We gathered data about what culture means to different groups, and about how GenAI should approach the representation of cultural artifacts, concepts, or values. We distill working definitions of culture directly from these communities to build an understanding of its conceptual complexities and how they relate to representations in Generative AI. We survey from across parts of Europe, North and South America, Asia, and Africa. We conclude with a set of recommendations for Culture and GenAI development. These include participatory approaches, prioritizing specific cultural dimensions beyond geography, such as religion and tradition, and a sensitivity framework for addressing cultural ``redlines''.

AI.tldr: The paper reports results from a large global survey on how people think generative AI should represent culture and proposes recommendations for culturally sensitive GenAI design.
AI.motivation: There is growing deployment of generative AI systems that depict and speak about different cultures, but there is little empirical, cross-cultural evidence on what diverse communities themselves believe culture is and how they want it represented by GenAI. This gap risks misrepresentation, cultural harm, and design choices driven only by developers’ assumptions.
AI.method: The authors conducted a large-scale global survey across multiple regions (parts of Europe, North and South America, Asia, and Africa). The survey asked participants what culture means to them, which dimensions of culture they consider important, and how they believe GenAI should handle cultural artifacts, concepts, and values. They then analyzed the responses to derive working definitions and dimensions of culture grounded in participants’ perspectives, and linked these to implications for GenAI representations.
AI.result: From the survey, the authors obtained empirically grounded definitions and dimensions of culture as understood by diverse populations. They identified which aspects (e.g., geography, religion, tradition, values, practices) people prioritize and how they think GenAI should treat cultural content. They uncovered conceptual complexities—for example, that culture is not only geographic or national but also tied to belief systems, traditions, and sensitive boundaries—and mapped these to expectations for GenAI’s behavior.
AI.conclusion: The paper concludes that GenAI development must explicitly account for culturally grounded perspectives gathered from affected communities. It recommends participatory design processes, giving more weight to cultural dimensions beyond geography (such as religion and traditions), and implementing a sensitivity framework to respect cultural “redlines” (boundaries of acceptable representation). Together, these steps aim to guide more respectful, accurate, and community-aligned cultural representation in generative AI systems.

title: Artificial Intelligence for Climate Adaptation: Reinforcement Learning for Climate Change-Resilient Transport
summary: Climate change is expected to intensify rainfall and, consequently, pluvial flooding, leading to increased disruptions in urban transportation systems over the coming decades. Designing effective adaptation strategies is challenging due to the long-term, sequential nature of infrastructure investments, deep climate uncertainty, and the complex interactions between flooding, infrastructure, and mobility impacts. In this work, we propose a novel decision-support framework using reinforcement learning (RL) for long-term flood adaptation planning. Formulated as an integrated assessment model (IAM), the framework combines rainfall projection and flood modeling, transport simulation, and quantification of direct and indirect impacts on infrastructure and mobility. Our RL-based approach learns adaptive strategies that balance investment and maintenance costs against avoided impacts. We evaluate the framework through a case study of Copenhagen's inner city over the 2024-2100 period, testing multiple adaptation options, and different belief and realized climate scenarios. Results show that the framework outperforms traditional optimization approaches by discovering coordinated spatial and temporal adaptation pathways and learning trade-offs between impact reduction and adaptation investment, yielding more resilient strategies. Overall, our results showcase the potential of reinforcement learning as a flexible decision-support tool for adaptive infrastructure planning under climate uncertainty.

AI.tldr: The paper presents a reinforcement learning-based decision-support framework to plan long-term urban flood adaptation strategies for transportation systems under climate uncertainty.
AI.motivation: Traditional planning and optimization approaches struggle with the long-term, sequential, and uncertain nature of climate adaptation investments for urban infrastructure, particularly where complex interactions between rainfall, flooding, transport networks, and mobility impacts must be considered. There is a need for tools that can design adaptive, flexible strategies that evolve over decades as climate conditions unfold.
AI.method: The authors formulate an integrated assessment model (IAM) that couples rainfall projection and flood modeling with transport simulation and impact quantification on infrastructure and mobility. Within this IAM, they embed a reinforcement learning (RL) agent that learns adaptation policies over time. The RL framework evaluates different spatial and temporal investment and maintenance decisions, under multiple climate belief and realization scenarios, to find strategies that minimize total costs, defined as a combination of adaptation costs and avoided flood-related impacts.
AI.result: Applied to Copenhagen's inner city from 2024 to 2100, the RL-based framework tests multiple adaptation options and climate scenarios. The learned policies outperform traditional optimization methods by discovering coordinated, staged adaptation pathways. These policies better manage the trade-off between up-front and ongoing investments versus reductions in direct and indirect flood impacts, resulting in more resilient transport infrastructure and mobility outcomes across a wide range of climate futures.
AI.conclusion: Reinforcement learning, when embedded in an integrated assessment modeling framework, can serve as a powerful and flexible decision-support tool for long-term, adaptive infrastructure planning under deep climate uncertainty. By capturing complex system interactions and learning sequential investment strategies, RL can generate more resilient and cost-effective flood adaptation plans than conventional optimization approaches.

title: Structured Multidimensional Representation Learning for Large Language Models
comment: 25 pages, 6 figures. Preprint of a journal submission
summary: Transformer architectures achieve state-of-the-art performance across a wide range of pattern recognition and natural language processing tasks, but their scaling is accompanied by substantial parameter growth and redundancy in the embedding dimension. In this work, we introduce a structured spectral factorization of the embedding space based on the L-product for third-order tensors. By reshaping token representations into spectral tensor slices and performing attention and feed-forward operations in the transform domain, we obtain a Tensor Transformer architecture that decomposes the encoder into p independent spectral sub-transformers while preserving standard Transformer semantics. We prove that the proposed L-Transformer is spectrally equivalent to p parallel Transformers operating on reduceddimensional embeddings, which yields approximately 1/p reduction (up to lower-order terms such as biases and normalization parameters) in encoder parameters under fixed total embedding size. When instantiated with a real-valued Discrete Cosine Transform (DCT), the method remains fully differentiable and compatible with existing training pipelines. Beyond compression, the spectral decomposition introduces an inductive bias over embedding frequencies, enabling slice-dependent frequency scaling that improves generalization. Experiments on IMDB and AG~News show that the proposed model can substantially reduce encoder parameters (up to 75\% for p=4) while maintaining competitive accuracy. On IMDB, the tensorized encoder matches or improves upon the standard baseline under compression, whereas on AG~News at moderate width we observe a small accuracy decrease in exchange for a 4 times encoder reduction; at BERT-base width (d=768), performance returns to parity.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a Tensor (L-)Transformer that factorizes the embedding space spectrally to reduce parameters by about 1/p while keeping similar accuracy.
AI.motivation: Standard Transformers scale up by increasing embedding size, which leads to large parameter counts and redundancy in the embedding dimension. There is a need for methods that compress or factorize the embedding space to reduce parameters while preserving performance and compatibility with existing training pipelines.
AI.method: They introduce a structured spectral factorization of the embedding space using the L-product for third-order tensors. Token embeddings are reshaped into spectral tensor slices, and attention and feed-forward operations are carried out in the transform (spectral) domain. This results in a Tensor Transformer (L-Transformer) that decomposes the encoder into p independent spectral sub-transformers. They instantiate the method with a real-valued Discrete Cosine Transform (DCT), keeping the model fully differentiable and plug-compatible with standard training. They also allow slice-dependent frequency scaling to exploit the frequency structure induced by the spectral decomposition.
AI.result: They theoretically show that the L-Transformer is spectrally equivalent to p parallel Transformers each operating on reduced-dimensional embeddings and achieves roughly a 1/p reduction in encoder parameters (ignoring lower-order terms).
