Empirically, on IMDB and AG News, they demonstrate encoder parameter reductions up to 75% for p=4 with competitive accuracy. On IMDB, the tensorized encoder matches or even improves over the baseline under compression; on AG News, there is a small accuracy drop at moderate width for a 4x encoder reduction, while at BERT-base width (d=768) performance matches the baseline.
AI.conclusion: Spectral tensorization of Transformer embeddings via the L-product offers an effective and theoretically grounded way to decompose the encoder into independent spectral sub-transformers, yielding large parameter savings with little or no loss in accuracy. The DCT-based implementation is compatible with existing pipelines and introduces a useful inductive bias over embedding frequencies that can improve generalization.

title: The EpisTwin: A Knowledge Graph-Grounded Neuro-Symbolic Architecture for Personal AI
summary: Personal Artificial Intelligence is currently hindered by the fragmentation of user data across isolated silos. While Retrieval-Augmented Generation offers a partial remedy, its reliance on unstructured vector similarity fails to capture the latent semantic topology and temporal dependencies essential for holistic sensemaking. We introduce EpisTwin, a neuro-symbolic framework that grounds generative reasoning in a verifiable, user-centric Personal Knowledge Graph. EpisTwin leverages Multimodal Language Models to lift heterogeneous, cross-application data into semantic triples. At inference, EpisTwin enables complex reasoning over the personal semantic graph via an agentic coordinator that combines Graph Retrieval-Augmented Generation with Online Deep Visual Refinement, dynamically re-grounding symbolic entities in their raw visual context. We also introduce PersonalQA-71-100, a synthetic benchmark designed to simulate a realistic user's digital footprint and evaluate EpisTwin performance. Our framework demonstrates robust results across a suite of state-of-the-art judge models, offering a promising direction for trustworthy Personal AI.

AI.tldr: EpisTwin is a neuro-symbolic framework that uses personal knowledge graphs built from multimodal user data to support more accurate and trustworthy personal AI reasoning.
AI.motivation: Personal AI systems struggle because user data is scattered across apps and current RAG methods using only vector similarity can’t fully capture semantic structure or temporal relationships, limiting holistic understanding and trustworthy reasoning.
AI.method: EpisTwin constructs a user-centric Personal Knowledge Graph by using multimodal language models to convert heterogeneous, cross-application data into semantic triples. At inference time, an agentic coordinator performs graph-based RAG combined with Online Deep Visual Refinement, which re-grounds symbolic entities in the original visual data during reasoning. They also create PersonalQA-71-100, a synthetic benchmark representing a realistic digital footprint to evaluate the system.
AI.result: EpisTwin achieves strong performance on the new PersonalQA-71-100 benchmark when evaluated by multiple state-of-the-art judge models, indicating better reasoning and trustworthiness compared to baselines (details not in abstract but implied).
AI.conclusion: Grounding generative reasoning in a verifiable personal knowledge graph, enriched with multimodal data and graph-based retrieval, is a promising path toward more trustworthy and capable Personal AI systems, as demonstrated by EpisTwin’s robust benchmark performance.

title: Let's Talk, Not Type: An Oral-First Multi-Agent Architecture for Guaraní
summary: Although artificial intelligence (AI) and Human-Computer Interaction (HCI) systems are often presented as universal solutions, their design remains predominantly text-first, underserving primarily oral languages and indigenous communities. This position paper uses Guaraní, an official and widely spoken language of Paraguay, as a case study to argue that language support in AI remains insufficient unless it aligns with lived oral practices. We propose an alternative to the standard "text-to-speech" pipeline, proposing instead an oral-first multi-agent architecture. By decoupling Guaraní natural language understanding from dedicated agents for conversation state and community-led governance, we demonstrate a technical framework that respects indigenous data sovereignty and diglossia. Our work moves beyond mere recognition to focus on turn-taking, repair, and shared context as the primary locus of interaction. We conclude that for AI to be truly culturally grounded, it must shift from adapting oral languages to text-centric systems to treating spoken conversation as a first-class design requirement, ensuring digital ecosystems empower rather than overlook diverse linguistic practices.

AI.tldr: The paper critiques text-first AI/HCI design for oral and indigenous languages, using Guaraní as a case study, and proposes an oral-first, multi-agent conversational architecture that aligns with lived oral practices and respects indigenous data sovereignty.
AI.motivation: AI and HCI systems typically assume a text-centric model of language, which marginalizes primarily oral languages and indigenous communities whose communication practices are not well represented by written text. Existing language support (e.g., adding text-to-speech) is insufficient because it does not align with the actual oral practices, interaction norms, or sociolinguistic realities such as diglossia and community governance over data. The authors aim to address this mismatch and advocate for culturally grounded AI that genuinely serves oral language communities.
AI.method: This is a position paper that uses Guaraní as a case study. The authors conceptually design and describe an oral-first multi-agent AI architecture. In this architecture, Guaraní natural language understanding is decoupled from agents handling conversation state and from community-led governance components. The framework emphasizes mechanisms for turn-taking, repair, and shared context, and incorporates principles of indigenous data sovereignty and sensitivity to diglossia. Rather than implementing a traditional text-to-speech pipeline, they propose a system that treats spoken conversation as the primary interface and organizational principle.
AI.result: The paper presents a technical and conceptual framework for an oral-first, multi-agent conversational AI system tailored to Guaraní, demonstrating how language understanding, dialogue management, and governance can be separated yet integrated. It shows how such an architecture can respect indigenous data sovereignty, accommodate diglossia, and better model interactional phenomena like turn-taking and repair. The result is a blueprint that illustrates how AI systems can move from simple recognition of oral languages to deeper, context-aware participation in oral conversational practices.
AI.conclusion: AI and HCI systems that simply retrofit oral languages into text-centric pipelines fail to provide culturally grounded support for indigenous and primarily oral communities. Instead, AI must treat spoken conversation—its turn-taking structures, repair mechanisms, and shared context—as a first-class design requirement. By adopting an oral-first, multi-agent architecture with explicit community governance and respect for indigenous data sovereignty, digital ecosystems can more effectively empower diverse linguistic practices rather than overlooking or distorting them.

title: SAHOO: Safeguarded Alignment for High-Order Optimization Objectives in Recursive Self-Improvement
comment: Published at ICLR 2026 Workshop on AI with Recursive Self-Improvement. 20 pages, 5 figures
summary: Recursive self-improvement is moving from theory to practice: modern systems can critique, revise, and evaluate their own outputs, yet iterative self-modification risks subtle alignment drift. We introduce SAHOO, a practical framework to monitor and control drift through three safeguards: (i) the Goal Drift Index (GDI), a learned multi-signal detector combining semantic, lexical, structural, and distributional measures; (ii) constraint preservation checks that enforce safety-critical invariants such as syntactic correctness and non-hallucination; and (iii) regression-risk quantification to flag improvement cycles that undo prior gains. Across 189 tasks in code generation, mathematical reasoning, and truthfulness, SAHOO produces substantial quality gains, including 18.3 percent improvement in code tasks and 16.8 percent in reasoning, while preserving constraints in two domains and maintaining low violations in truthfulness. Thresholds are calibrated on a small validation set of 18 tasks across three cycles. We further map the capability-alignment frontier, showing efficient early improvement cycles but rising alignment costs later and exposing domain-specific tensions such as fluency versus factuality. SAHOO therefore makes alignment preservation during recursive self-improvement measurable, deployable, and systematically validated at scale.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes SAHOO, a framework that monitors and limits alignment drift during recursive self-improvement in AI systems while still achieving performance gains.
AI.motivation: As AI systems begin to iteratively critique and modify their own outputs, they can improve performance but also risk gradual misalignment with intended goals, safety constraints, or truthfulness. There is a lack of practical, quantitative tools to track and control this alignment drift during recursive self-improvement processes. The paper aims to provide a framework that makes such drift measurable and manageable in realistic settings.
AI.method: The authors introduce SAHOO, a framework composed of three safeguards. First, the Goal Drift Index (GDI) is a learned multi-signal detector that aggregates semantic, lexical, structural, and distributional indicators of drift from target behavior. Second, constraint preservation checks enforce safety-critical invariants such as syntactic validity in code and avoidance of hallucinations in text. Third, regression-risk quantification monitors whether new self-improvement cycles undo prior quality gains. The system is evaluated across 189 tasks in code generation, mathematical reasoning, and truthfulness, with thresholds tuned on a small validation set over three self-improvement cycles and used to study the trade-offs between capability gains and alignment preservation.
AI.result: SAHOO achieves substantial performance gains across diverse domains while maintaining alignment constraints. It yields an 18.3% improvement on code tasks and a 16.8% improvement on mathematical reasoning, preserves constraints effectively in two evaluated domains, and keeps constraint violations low in truthfulness tasks. The calibrated thresholds enable practical deployment across multiple self-improvement cycles and reveal how alignment and capability evolve jointly over time.
AI.conclusion: SAHOO offers a deployable, empirically validated way to monitor and control alignment drift in recursively self-improving systems. By combining a learned Goal Drift Index, explicit constraint checks, and regression-risk assessment, it allows significant performance improvements while keeping alignment degradation measurable and bounded. The analysis of the capability-alignment frontier highlights that early improvement cycles are efficient but later cycles incur growing alignment costs and domain-specific trade-offs, making SAHOO a useful tool for responsible scaling of recursive self-improvement.

title: CodeScout: Contextual Problem Statement Enhancement for Software Agents
summary: Current AI-powered code assistance tools often struggle with poorly-defined problem statements that lack sufficient task context and requirements specification. Recent analysis of software engineering agents reveals that failures on such underspecified requests are highly correlated with longer trajectories involving either over-exploration or repeated attempts at applying the same fix without proper evolution or testing, leading to suboptimal outcomes across software development tasks. We introduce CodeScout, a contextual query refinement approach that systematically converts underspecified user requests into comprehensive, actionable problem statements through lightweight pre-exploration of the target codebase. Our key innovation is demonstrating that structured analysis before task execution can supplement existing agentic capabilities without requiring any modifications to their underlying scaffolds. CodeScout performs targeted context scoping, conducts multi-perspective analysis examining potential fixes and exploration opportunities, then synthesizes these insights into enhanced problem statements with reproduction steps, expected behaviors, and targeted exploration hints. This pre-exploration directly addresses the identified failure patterns by reducing non-converging agent trajectories while clarifying user intent in natural language space. We evaluate CodeScout using state-of-the-art agentic scaffolds and language models on SWEBench-Verified, demonstrating a 20\% improvement in resolution rates with up to 27 additional issues resolved compared to the default baseline method. Our results suggest that systematic query refinement through contextual analysis represents a promising direction for enhancing AI code assistance capabilities.

AI.tldr: The paper presents CodeScout, a system that improves AI code assistants by turning vague bug-fix or feature requests into precise, well-specified tasks via lightweight codebase pre-exploration, leading to significantly better success rates on software engineering benchmarks.
AI.motivation: AI code assistants and software engineering agents frequently fail when user requests are vague or underspecified, causing long, non-converging action trajectories with over-exploration or repeated, ineffective fixes. There is a need for a method that clarifies task context and requirements without redesigning existing agent frameworks, so that agents can better understand and solve coding tasks from real-world issue reports.
AI.method: The authors propose CodeScout, a contextual query refinement pipeline that operates before the main agent executes. Given an underspecified request, CodeScout: (1) scopes relevant parts of the codebase; (2) performs multi-perspective pre-exploration, considering possible fixes and exploration paths; and (3) synthesizes an enriched problem statement that includes reproduction steps, expected behavior, and targeted exploration hints. This refined statement is then fed into existing agentic scaffolds and LLMs without modifying their internals.
AI.result: On the SWEBench-Verified benchmark, integrating CodeScout with state-of-the-art agentic scaffolds and language models yields a 20% improvement in issue resolution rates, resolving up to 27 more issues compared to the baseline agents that do not use contextual query refinement. The improved performance is linked to shorter, more convergent trajectories and clearer task specifications.
AI.conclusion: Systematic query refinement through lightweight contextual analysis of the codebase, as implemented in CodeScout, effectively mitigates common failure modes of AI code assistants on underspecified tasks. By enhancing the initial problem formulation without altering existing agent architectures, this approach significantly boosts resolution rates and represents a promising path for more reliable AI-powered software development assistance.

title: Talk Freely, Execute Strictly: Schema-Gated Agentic AI for Flexible and Reproducible Scientific Workflows
summary:
Large language models (LLMs) can now translate a researcher's plain-language goal into executable computation, yet scientific workflows demand determinism, provenance, and governance that are difficult to guarantee when an LLM decides what runs. Semi-structured interviews with 18 experts across 10 industrial R&D stakeholders surface 2 competing requirements--deterministic, constrained execution and conversational flexibility without workflow rigidity--together with boundary properties (human-in-the-loop control and transparency) that any resolution must satisfy. We propose schema-gated orchestration as the resolving principle: the schema becomes a mandatory execution boundary at the composed-workflow level, so that nothing runs unless the complete action--including cross-step dependencies--validates against a machine-checkable specification.
  We operationalize the 2 requirements as execution determinism (ED) and conversational flexibility (CF), and use these axes to review 20 systems spanning 5 architectural groups along a validation-scope spectrum. Scores are assigned via a multi-model protocol--15 independent sessions across 3 LLM families--yielding substantial-to-near-perfect inter-model agreement (Krippendorff a=0.80 for ED and a=0.98 for CF), demonstrating that multi-model LLM scoring can serve as a reusable alternative to human expert panels for architectural assessment.
  The resulting landscape reveals an empirical Pareto front--no reviewed system achieves both high flexibility and high determinism--but a convergence zone emerges between the generative and workflow-centric extremes. We argue that a schema-gated architecture, separating conversational from execution authority, is positioned to decouple this trade-off, and distill 3 operational principles--clarification-before-execution, constrained plan-act orchestration, and tool-to-workflow-level gating--to guide adoption.

AI.tldr: The paper studies how to make LLM-driven scientific workflows both deterministic and flexible, proposes “schema-gated orchestration” as a design principle, and evaluates existing systems along determinism and conversational flexibility using multi-model LLM-based scoring.
AI.motivation: LLMs can translate natural-language research goals into executable steps, but scientific and industrial R&D workflows require strong guarantees about what exactly runs (determinism, provenance, governance). Existing LLM agents and workflow tools either give too much execution freedom to the model (reducing safety and reproducibility) or enforce rigid pipelines (reducing flexibility and usefulness in open-ended research settings). The authors want a principled way to reconcile these competing needs and to systematically compare architectures in this design space.
AI.method: 1) Conduct semi-structured interviews with 18 experts from 10 industrial R&D organizations to elicit requirements and boundary conditions for LLM-based workflows. 2) Formalize two key axes: execution determinism (ED) and conversational flexibility (CF). 3) Review 20 existing systems, grouped into 5 architectural families, and assess them along ED and CF using a multi-model protocol: 15 independent evaluation sessions across 3 different LLM families. 4) Quantify inter-model agreement with Krippendorff’s alpha to validate that LLM ensembles can reliably approximate expert architectural assessments. 5) Analyze the resulting landscape to identify trade-offs and promising regions, and propose “schema-gated orchestration” and three operational principles as a target architecture.
AI.result: The LLM-based scoring of 20 systems across 5 architectural groups shows high inter-model agreement (Krippendorff’s alpha 0.80 for ED and 0.98 for CF), suggesting that multi-model LLM evaluation can stand in for expert panels for this kind of architectural survey. The resulting landscape uncovers a Pareto frontier: no existing system simultaneously achieves high execution determinism and high conversational flexibility. However, the data show a convergence region between purely generative agents and rigid workflow systems, hinting at a viable compromise architecture. The analysis further clarifies how different validation scopes and orchestration strategies place systems along the ED–CF spectrum.
AI.conclusion: Current LLM-driven workflow systems face a fundamental-seeming trade-off between determinism and conversational flexibility, and none fully resolves it. The authors argue that “schema-gated orchestration”—where machine-checkable schemas are enforced at the composed-workflow level and nothing executes unless the full cross-step action validates against this schema—can separate conversational interaction from execution authority and thereby relax this trade-off. They distill three practical principles—clarification-before-execution, constrained plan–act orchestration, and gating at the tool-to-workflow boundary—to guide the design of future systems that better satisfy scientific needs for control, transparency, and flexibility. They also demonstrate that multi-model LLM scoring is a promising, reusable method for architectural evaluation in this domain.

title: NERdME: a Named Entity Recognition Dataset for Indexing Research Artifacts in Code Repositories
summary: Existing scholarly information extraction (SIE) datasets focus on scientific papers and overlook implementation-level details in code repositories. README files describe datasets, source code, and other implementation-level artifacts, however, their free-form Markdown offers little semantic structure, making automatic information extraction difficult. To address this gap, NERdME is introduced: 200 manually annotated README files with over 10,000 labeled spans and 10 entity types. Baseline results using large language models and fine-tuned transformers show clear differences between paperlevel and implementation-level entities, indicating the value of extending SIE benchmarks with entity types available in README files. A downstream entity-linking experiment was conducted to demonstrate that entities derived from READMEs can support artifact discovery and metadata integration.

AI.tldr: The paper presents NERdME, a new annotated dataset of GitHub README files for scholarly information extraction, showing that implementation-level entities differ from paper-level ones and can aid artifact discovery.
AI.motivation: Existing scholarly information extraction focuses on scientific papers and misses implementation-level details contained in code repositories. README files describe datasets, code, and other artifacts but are written in unstructured Markdown, making automatic extraction of useful metadata difficult. There is a need for a structured benchmark capturing these implementation-level entities to extend SIE beyond papers.
AI.method: The authors manually annotated 200 README files with over 10,000 labeled spans across 10 entity types to create the NERdME dataset. They then trained and evaluated baseline systems, including large language models and fine-tuned transformer models, on this dataset to perform named entity recognition. They compared performance on paper-level versus implementation-level entities and also ran a downstream entity-linking experiment using the extracted entities.
AI.result: The baselines show that implementation-level entities from README files behave differently from traditional paper-level entities, validating the need for separate modeling and benchmarks. The entity-linking experiment demonstrates that entities extracted from READMEs can be successfully linked to external resources, supporting tasks like artifact discovery and metadata integration.
AI.conclusion: NERdME fills a gap in scholarly information extraction by providing a dedicated dataset for implementation-level entities found in code repository READMEs. Differences between paper-level and implementation-level entities justify extending SIE benchmarks, and the positive downstream entity-linking results indicate that leveraging README-derived entities can improve discovery and integration of research artifacts.

title: Boosting deep Reinforcement Learning using pretraining with Logical Options
summary: Deep reinforcement learning agents are often misaligned, as they over-exploit early reward signals. Recently, several symbolic approaches have addressed these challenges by encoding sparse objectives along with aligned plans. However, purely symbolic architectures are complex to scale and difficult to apply to continuous settings. Hence, we propose a hybrid approach, inspired by humans' ability to acquire new skills. We use a two-stage framework that injects symbolic structure into neural-based reinforcement learning agents without sacrificing the expressivity of deep policies. Our method, called Hybrid Hierarchical RL (H^2RL), introduces a logical option-based pretraining strategy to steer the learning policy away from short-term reward loops and toward goal-directed behavior while allowing the final policy to be refined via standard environment interaction. Empirically, we show that this approach consistently improves long-horizon decision-making and yields agents that outperform strong neural, symbolic, and neuro-symbolic baselines.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes Hybrid Hierarchical RL (H^2RL), a two-stage hybrid symbolic–neural framework that uses logical, option-based pretraining to avoid short-term reward hacking and improve long-horizon, goal-directed behavior, outperforming neural, symbolic, and neuro-symbolic baselines.
AI.motivation: Deep RL agents tend to become misaligned by over-exploiting early or dense reward signals, often getting stuck in short-term reward loops instead of pursuing task-aligned, long-horizon goals. Symbolic methods can express sparse, goal-oriented objectives and plans, but they are hard to scale and to apply in continuous domains. There is a need for a method that combines symbolic structure with the expressive power and scalability of deep RL, to achieve better alignment and long-horizon performance without giving up the strengths of neural policies.
AI.method: The authors propose Hybrid Hierarchical RL (H^2RL), a two-stage hybrid learning framework. In the first stage, the agent is pretrained using symbolic structure in the form of logical options (temporally extended actions derived from symbolic objectives and plans). These structured options guide the agent away from exploiting short-term rewards and toward executing meaningful, goal-directed behaviors. In the second stage, the agent continues to learn via standard environment interaction, allowing the neural policy to refine and adapt beyond the initial symbolic scaffolding. The architecture preserves the expressivity of deep policies while injecting symbolic hierarchy through option-based pretraining.
AI.result: Empirical evaluations show that H^2RL consistently improves performance on long-horizon decision-making tasks, indicating better ability to handle delayed rewards and complex objectives. Across benchmarks, agents trained with H^2RL outperform strong baselines, including purely neural RL methods, purely symbolic planners, and existing neuro-symbolic approaches, demonstrating both improved alignment and superior task performance.
AI.conclusion: Hybrid Hierarchical RL successfully combines symbolic structure with deep reinforcement learning by using logical options for pretraining and then refining policies through standard interaction. This hybrid strategy mitigates short-term reward exploitation, enhances goal-directed, long-horizon behavior, and yields state-of-the-art performance compared to neural, symbolic, and neuro-symbolic baselines. The work suggests that structured symbolic priors can effectively steer deep RL towards more aligned and robust behaviors without sacrificing expressivity or scalability.

title: PVminerLLM: Structured Extraction of Patient Voice from Patient-Generated Text using Large Language Models
summary:
Motivation: Patient-generated text contains critical information about patients' lived experiences, social circumstances, and engagement in care, including factors that strongly influence adherence, care coordination, and health equity. However, these patient voice signals are rarely available in structured form, limiting their use in patient-centered outcomes research and clinical quality improvement. Reliable extraction of such information is therefore essential for understanding and addressing non-clinical drivers of health outcomes at scale.
  Results: We introduce PVminer, a benchmark for structured extraction of patient voice, and propose PVminerLLM, a supervised fine-tuned large language model tailored to this task. Across multiple datasets and model sizes, PVminerLLM substantially outperforms prompt-based baselines, achieving up to 83.82% F1 for Code prediction, 80.74% F1 for Sub-code prediction, and 87.03% F1 for evidence Span extraction. Notably, strong performance is achieved even with smaller models, demonstrating that reliable patient voice extraction is feasible without extreme model scale. These results enable scalable analysis of social and experiential signals embedded in patient-generated text.
  Availability and Implementation: Code, evaluation scripts, and trained LLMs will be released publicly. Annotated datasets will be made available upon request for research use.
  Keywords: Large Language Models, Supervised Fine-Tuning, Medical Annotation, Patient-Generated Text, Clinical NLP

AI.tldr: PVminer introduces a benchmark and fine-tuned LLM (PVminerLLM) for extracting structured ‘patient voice’ signals from patient-generated text, achieving strong F1 scores for code, sub-code, and evidence span prediction, even with smaller models.
AI.motivation: Patient-generated text contains rich information about patients’ lived experiences, social and contextual factors, and engagement with care—key drivers of adherence, coordination, and equity—but this “patient voice” is rarely available in structured form, which limits its use in patient-centered research and clinical quality improvement. A reliable method is needed to extract and structure these signals at scale.
AI.method: The authors construct PVminer, a benchmark task for structured extraction of patient voice from patient-generated text, and develop PVminerLLM, a supervised fine-tuned large language model optimized for this task. They compare PVminerLLM, in multiple model sizes, against prompt-based LLM baselines on code prediction, sub-code prediction, and evidence span extraction.
AI.result: PVminerLLM substantially outperforms prompt-based baselines across datasets and model sizes, reaching up to 83.82% F1 for code prediction, 80.74% F1 for sub-code prediction, and 87.03% F1 for evidence span extraction. Smaller model variants still perform strongly, indicating that accurate patient voice extraction does not require very large models.
AI.conclusion: Structured extraction of patient voice from patient-generated text is feasible and effective using supervised fine-tuned LLMs, even at smaller scales. PVminer and PVminerLLM provide a foundation for scalable analysis of social and experiential patient signals, supporting patient-centered outcomes research and clinical quality improvement. Code and models will be publicly available, and annotated data will be shareable for research use.

title: Tutor Move Taxonomy: A Theory-Aligned Framework for Analyzing Instructional Moves in Tutoring
summary: Understanding what makes tutoring effective requires methods for systematically analyzing tutors' instructional actions during learning interactions. This paper presents a tutor move taxonomy designed to support large-scale analysis of tutoring dialogue within the National Tutoring Observatory. The taxonomy provides a structured annotation framework for labeling tutors' instructional moves during one-on-one tutoring sessions. We developed the taxonomy through a hybrid deductive-inductive process. First, we synthesized research from cognitive science, the learning sciences, classroom discourse analysis, and intelligent tutoring systems to construct a preliminary framework of tutoring moves. We then refined the taxonomy through iterative coding of authentic tutoring transcripts conducted by expert annotators with extensive instructional and qualitative research experience. The resulting taxonomy organizes tutoring behaviors into four categories: tutoring support, learning support, social-emotional and motivational support, and logistical support. Learning support moves are further organized along a spectrum of student engagement, distinguishing between moves that elicit student reasoning and those that provide direct explanation or answers. By defining tutoring dialogue in terms of discrete instructional actions, the taxonomy enables scalable annotation using AI, computational modeling of tutoring strategies, and empirical analysis of how tutoring behaviors relate to learning outcomes.

AI.tldr: A paper introduces a tutor move taxonomy to systematically code and analyze tutors' instructional actions in one-on-one tutoring sessions, enabling large-scale, AI-supported study of how specific tutoring behaviors relate to learning outcomes.
AI.motivation: Effective tutoring is known to improve learning, but researchers lack scalable, systematic ways to analyze what tutors actually do during tutoring sessions and which specific instructional actions drive learning gains. Existing frameworks from related fields are either not tailored to one-on-one tutoring dialogue or not easily scalable for large datasets such as those in the National Tutoring Observatory.
AI.method: The authors used a hybrid deductive-inductive approach. They began by synthesizing theories and findings from cognitive science, learning sciences, classroom discourse analysis, and intelligent tutoring systems to create an initial framework of tutoring moves. They then iteratively refined and reorganized this framework through repeated coding of authentic one-on-one tutoring transcripts by expert annotators experienced in instruction and qualitative analysis, adjusting categories and definitions based on coding practice.
AI.result: They produced a structured tutor move taxonomy that categorizes tutors' behaviors into four main types: tutoring support, learning support, social-emotional and motivational support, and logistical support. Within learning support, moves are organized along a spectrum of student engagement from eliciting student reasoning to providing direct explanation or answers. The taxonomy is formulated so that tutors' dialogue can be represented as sequences of discrete, codable instructional actions.
AI.conclusion: This taxonomy offers a foundation for large-scale, systematic analysis of tutoring dialogue. By defining and operationalizing discrete tutoring moves, it supports AI-based annotation, computational modeling of tutoring strategies, and empirical research on the relationships between specific tutoring behaviors and student learning outcomes, thereby advancing the science and practice of effective tutoring.

title: RouteGoT: Node-Adaptive Routing for Cost-Efficient Graph of Thoughts Reasoning
summary: Large Language Models (LLMs) excel at multi-step reasoning, yet increasing the structural complexity of inference does not consistently improve system-level returns. Methods such as Tree of Thoughts (ToT), Graph of Thoughts (GoT), and Adaptive Graph of Thoughts (AGoT) can boost accuracy on some benchmarks, but often introduce substantial overhead in token consumption and latency, and their gains can be unstable across task distributions-sometimes underperforming simpler Chain-of-Thought (CoT) or direct input-output prompting (IO). We attribute this inefficiency to stage-wise and node-wise heterogeneity inside GoT-style reasoning pipelines: high-quality planning and final synthesis are globally coupled and typically benefit from strong models, whereas many intermediate subtasks are localized and can be solved accurately by lighter models with far fewer tokens. Motivated by these observations, we propose RouteGoT, a budget-controllable, node-adaptive routing framework for graph-structured reasoning. RouteGoT performs in-graph routing by prioritizing strong models for planning and synthesis, while dynamically allocating lightweight models and cost-effective strategies to leaf subtasks based on predicted difficulty. It further integrates explicit budget constraints into a global inference scheduler to control graph expansion under a user-specified token budget, enabling predictable performance-cost trade-offs. Experiments across reasoning, retrieval, and multi-hop QA benchmarks show that RouteGoT matching or improving accuracy while substantially reducing token usage; specifically, it achieves an average 8.1 percentage points accuracy improvement and 79.1\% output token reduction compared to AGoT. Furthermore, RouteGoT outperforms existing routing baselines by maintaining a superior cost-accuracy trade-off, demonstrating improved robustness under varying budget targets and tasks.

AI.tldr: RouteGoT is a routing framework that uses strong LLMs only for globally important reasoning nodes and cheaper models for local subtasks in graph-based reasoning, achieving better accuracy–cost trade-offs under explicit token budgets.
AI.motivation: Existing graph-based reasoning methods like Tree/Graph/Adaptive Graph of Thoughts can increase accuracy but often waste tokens and time by uniformly using large models across all nodes, even when many intermediate subtasks are easy. Their performance gains are unstable across tasks, and they lack principled control of token budgets. The authors want a method that preserves or improves accuracy while cutting cost, with predictable performance under user-specified token limits.
AI.method: They introduce RouteGoT, a budget-aware, node-adaptive routing framework for GoT-style reasoning. It performs in-graph routing, assigning strong models to planning and final synthesis nodes, and lighter models or cheaper strategies to leaf/local subtasks based on predicted node difficulty. A global inference scheduler explicitly incorporates user token budgets to regulate graph expansion and model choices, balancing accuracy and cost throughout the reasoning graph.
AI.result: On reasoning, retrieval, and multi-hop QA benchmarks, RouteGoT matches or improves the accuracy of prior GoT-style methods while greatly reducing token usage. Relative to AGoT, it yields an average +8.1 percentage point accuracy gain and a 79.1% reduction in output tokens, and it surpasses routing baselines in cost–accuracy trade-off and robustness across different tasks and budget settings.
AI.conclusion: Adaptive, budget-controlled routing over graph-structured reasoning allows LLM systems to selectively apply strong models where global reasoning is needed, while using cheaper models for local subtasks. This leads to more robust and efficient multi-step reasoning, delivering better or comparable accuracy at substantially lower token cost and with predictable budget-aware behavior.

title: HART: Data-Driven Hallucination Attribution and Evidence-Based Tracing for Large Language Models
summary: Large language models (LLMs) have demonstrated remarkable performance in text generation and knowledge-intensive question answering. Nevertheless, they are prone to producing hallucinated content, which severely undermines their reliability in high-stakes application domains. Existing hallucination attribution approaches, based on either external knowledge retrieval or internal model mechanisms, primarily focus on semantic similarity matching or representation-level discrimination. As a result, they have difficulty establishing structured correspondences at the span level between hallucination types, underlying error generation mechanisms, and external factual evidence, thereby limiting the interpretability of hallucinated fragments and the traceability of supporting or opposing evidence. To address these limitations, we propose HART, a fine-grained hallucination attribution and evidence retrieval framework for large language models. HART formalizes hallucination tracing as a structured modeling task comprising four stages: span localization, mechanism attribution, evidence retrieval, and causal tracing. Based upon this formulation, we develop the first structured dataset tailored for hallucination tracing, in which hallucination types, error mechanisms, and sets of counterfactual evidence are jointly annotated to enable causal-level interpretability evaluation. Experimental results on the proposed dataset demonstrate that HART substantially outperforms strong retrieval baselines, including BM25 and DPR, validating the effectiveness and generalization capability of the proposed tracing paradigm for hallucination analysis and evidence alignment.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces HART, a structured framework to precisely detect and explain hallucinations in large language models, linking hallucinated spans to their error mechanisms and supporting/refuting evidence.
AI.motivation: Although large language models perform well on many tasks, their tendency to hallucinate makes them unreliable in high-stakes settings. Existing methods either rely on external retrieval or internal signals and mostly use coarse semantic similarity or representation-level checks. These approaches struggle to: (1) pinpoint hallucinations at the span level, (2) distinguish different hallucination types and underlying error mechanisms, and (3) connect those errors to concrete external evidence. This limits how interpretable and trustworthy hallucination analyses are.
AI.method: The authors propose HART, a fine-grained hallucination attribution and evidence retrieval framework. HART treats hallucination tracing as a structured, multi-stage task with four steps: (1) span localization – identify exact text spans that are hallucinated; (2) mechanism attribution – assign each hallucinated span to a specific hallucination type and error-generation mechanism; (3) evidence retrieval – retrieve external factual documents that support or contradict the span; and (4) causal tracing – connect hallucinations, mechanisms, and evidence into a causal explanation. They also construct a new structured dataset where hallucination types, mechanisms, and counterfactual evidence sets are jointly annotated, enabling evaluation of causal-level interpretability.
AI.result: On their new hallucination tracing dataset, HART significantly outperforms strong retrieval-based baselines such as BM25 and DPR in tasks related to hallucination attribution and evidence alignment. This shows that explicitly structured modeling of hallucination tracing is more effective than standard retrieval methods. The framework demonstrates good generalization across different hallucination analysis and evidence retrieval settings.
AI.conclusion: Structured, multi-stage modeling of hallucination tracing—spanning span detection, mechanism attribution, and evidence retrieval—improves both performance and interpretability for analyzing LLM hallucinations. HART offers a more fine-grained and causally grounded way to connect hallucinated content with its underlying generation mechanisms and external factual evidence, and the accompanying dataset provides a benchmark for future work on hallucination analysis and attribution.

title: Lost in Stories: Consistency Bugs in Long Story Generation by LLMs
summary: What happens when a storyteller forgets its own story? Large Language Models (LLMs) can now generate narratives spanning tens of thousands of words, but they often fail to maintain consistency throughout. When generating long-form narratives, these models can contradict their own established facts, character traits, and world rules. Existing story generation benchmarks focus mainly on plot quality and fluency, leaving consistency errors largely unexplored. To address this gap, we present ConStory-Bench, a benchmark designed to evaluate narrative consistency in long-form story generation. It contains 2,000 prompts across four task scenarios and defines a taxonomy of five error categories with 19 fine-grained subtypes. We also develop ConStory-Checker, an automated pipeline that detects contradictions and grounds each judgment in explicit textual evidence. Evaluating a range of LLMs through five research questions, we find that consistency errors show clear tendencies: they are most common in factual and temporal dimensions, tend to appear around the middle of narratives, occur in text segments with higher token-level entropy, and certain error types tend to co-occur. These findings can inform future efforts to improve consistency in long-form narrative generation. Our project page is available at https://picrew.github.io/constory-bench.github.io/.

AI.tldr: Introduces ConStory-Bench and ConStory-Checker to systematically evaluate and automatically detect consistency errors in long-form stories generated by large language models.
AI.motivation: Long-form story generation by LLMs can reach tens of thousands of words but frequently suffers from inconsistencies in facts, character traits, and world rules. Existing benchmarks focus on plot quality and fluency and largely ignore consistency, leaving a major quality dimension underexplored and making it hard to diagnose and improve models’ narrative reliability.
AI.method: The authors construct ConStory-Bench, a benchmark of 2,000 prompts spanning four narrative task scenarios, and define a taxonomy of five high-level consistency error categories with 19 more fine-grained subtypes. They then build ConStory-Checker, an automated evaluation pipeline that detects contradictions in generated stories and grounds each judgment in explicit supporting text spans. Using this setup, they run systematic experiments on multiple LLMs to answer five research questions about where and how consistency errors arise.
AI.result: Across models, consistency errors show systematic patterns: they most often concern factual and temporal aspects of stories, they appear disproportionately in the middle sections of narratives, they correlate with segments of higher token-level entropy (i.e., higher uncertainty in generation), and some error subtypes are more likely to co-occur than others. These empirical findings characterize the landscape of narrative inconsistencies in current LLMs.
AI.conclusion: ConStory-Bench and ConStory-Checker provide a focused framework for measuring and analyzing narrative consistency in long-form story generation. The observed error patterns highlight common failure modes and offer concrete signals—such as narrative position, uncertainty, and error co-occurrence—that can guide future model and decoding strategies aimed at producing more self-consistent long texts.

title: ReflexiCoder: Teaching Large Language Models to Self-Reflect on Generated Code and Self-Correct It via Reinforcement Learning
summary: While Large Language Models (LLMs) have revolutionized code generation, standard "System 1" approaches, generating solutions in a single forward pass, often hit a performance ceiling when faced with complex algorithmic tasks. Existing iterative refinement strategies attempt to bridge this gap at inference time, yet they predominantly rely on external oracles, execution feedback, or computationally expensive prompt-response cycles. In this work, we propose ReflexiCoder, a novel reinforcement learning (RL) framework that internalizes the structured reasoning trajectory, encompassing initial generation, bug and optimization aware reflection, and self-correction, directly into the model's weights. Unlike prior methods, ReflexiCoder shifts the paradigm from external-dependent refinement to an intrinsic, fully autonomous self-reflection and self-correction capabilities at inference time. We utilize an RL-zero training paradigm with granular reward functions to optimize the entire reflection-correction trajectory, teaching the model how to debug without reliance on ground-truth feedback or execution engines at inference time. Extensive experiments across seven benchmarks demonstrate that our ReflexiCoder-8B establishes a new state-of-the-art (SOTA) among leading open-source models in the 1.5B-14B range, achieving 94.51% (87.20%) on HumanEval (Plus), 81.80% (78.57%) on MBPP (Plus), 35.00% on BigCodeBench, 52.21% on LiveCodeBench, and 37.34% on CodeForces in a single-attempt setting, rivaling or surpassing proprietary models like GPT-5.1. Notably, our framework is significantly more token-efficient than base models, reducing inference-time compute overhead by approximately 40% through disciplined, high-speed reasoning and reflection patterns. Source code is available at https://github.com/juyongjiang/ReflexiCoder.

AI.tldr: ReflexiCoder is an RL-trained code LLM that learns an internal, multi-step debug-and-reflect process, achieving new SOTA code generation with fewer inference tokens.
AI.motivation: Standard one-shot (“System 1”) LLM code generation struggles on complex algorithmic tasks, and current iterative-refinement methods depend on external oracles, execution feedback, or costly multi-round prompting at inference time. The authors want a way for a model to perform effective multi-step reasoning, debugging, and optimization autonomously, without external tools or large compute overhead during inference.
AI.method: They introduce ReflexiCoder, a reinforcement learning framework that encodes a structured reasoning trajectory—initial code generation, reflection focused on bugs and optimization opportunities, and subsequent self-correction—directly into the model weights. Using an RL-zero–style setup with carefully designed granular rewards, they optimize this entire reflection–correction trajectory so the model learns to debug and refine its own code without needing ground-truth labels or execution engines at inference time. The core idea is to turn what is usually an external, multi-step refinement workflow into an internalized, single-model policy over reasoning steps.
AI.result: On seven code benchmarks, an 8B-parameter ReflexiCoder model sets new SOTA performance among open-source models in the 1.5B–14B range. It achieves 94.51% (87.20%) on HumanEval (Plus), 81.80% (78.57%) on MBPP (Plus), 35.00% on BigCodeBench, 52.21% on LiveCodeBench, and 37.34% on CodeForces in single-attempt evaluation, often rivaling or beating proprietary models such as GPT-5.1. In addition, its internalized reasoning leads to about a 40% reduction in inference-time token usage compared to base models, indicating more compute-efficient code reasoning and refinement.
AI.conclusion: Internalizing a structured reflect-and-correct trajectory via RL enables LLMs to autonomously debug and optimize code at inference time, removing reliance on external feedback or heavy multi-round prompting. ReflexiCoder demonstrates that such RL-zero training can yield both higher code-generation accuracy and significantly better token efficiency, suggesting a promising direction for future reasoning-focused LLM training and deployment.

title: Addressing the Ecological Fallacy in Larger LMs with Human Context
summary: Language model training and inference ignore a fundamental linguistic fact -- there is a dependence between multiple sequences of text written by the same person. Prior work has shown that addressing this form of \textit{ecological fallacy} can greatly improve the performance of multiple smaller (~124M) GPT-based models. In this work, we ask if addressing the ecological fallacy by modeling the author's language context with a specific LM task (called HuLM) can provide similar benefits for a larger-scale model, an 8B Llama model. To this end, we explore variants that process an author's language in the context of their other temporally ordered texts. We study the effect of pre-training with this author context using the HuLM objective, as well as using it during fine-tuning with author context (\textit{HuFT:Human-aware Fine-Tuning}). Empirical comparisons show that addressing the ecological fallacy during fine-tuning alone using QLoRA improves the performance of the larger 8B model over standard fine-tuning. Additionally, QLoRA-based continued HuLM pre-training results in a human-aware model generalizable for improved performance over eight downstream tasks with linear task classifier training alone. These results indicate the utility and importance of modeling language in the context of its original generators, the authors.

AI.tldr: The paper shows that incorporating author-specific language context (addressing the ecological fallacy) into training and fine-tuning improves performance of an 8B Llama model across multiple downstream tasks.
AI.motivation: Standard language model training treats texts as independent, ignoring that multiple texts from the same author are linguistically dependent. Prior work showed leveraging this author dependence helps smaller models, but it is unknown whether and how this scales to larger models like an 8B Llama.
AI.method: The authors define and use a human-aware LM task (HuLM) that explicitly models an author's language in the context of their temporally ordered texts. They investigate (1) continued pre-training with the HuLM objective on author-contextualized data, and (2) Human-aware Fine-Tuning (HuFT) where fine-tuning incorporates author context, implemented with parameter-efficient QLoRA on an 8B Llama model. They then evaluate these variants on multiple downstream tasks, primarily using linear classifiers on frozen representations.
AI.result: Fine-tuning that accounts for author context (HuFT with QLoRA) outperforms standard fine-tuning of the 8B model. Moreover, continuing HuLM-style pre-training with QLoRA yields a human-aware model that generalizes better, achieving improved performance over eight downstream tasks, even when only linear task classifiers are trained on top.
AI.conclusion: Modeling language in the context of its original generators—the authors—remains beneficial at larger model scales. Addressing the ecological fallacy via HuLM in pre-training and HuFT in fine-tuning enhances downstream performance, indicating that incorporating author-level context is a valuable and scalable direction for language model training.

title: ROSE: Reordered SparseGPT for More Accurate One-Shot Large Language Models Pruning
summary: Pruning is widely recognized as an effective method for reducing the parameters of large language models (LLMs), potentially leading to more efficient deployment and inference. One classic and prominent path of LLM one-shot pruning is to leverage second-order gradients (i.e., Hessian), represented by the pioneering work SparseGPT. However, the predefined left-to-right pruning order in SparseGPT leads to suboptimal performance when the weights exhibit columnar patterns. This paper studies the effect of pruning order under the SparseGPT framework. The analyses lead us to propose ROSE, a reordered SparseGPT method that prioritizes weights with larger potential pruning errors to be pruned earlier. ROSE first performs pre-pruning to identify candidate weights for removal, and estimates both column and block pruning loss. Subsequently, two-level reordering is performed: columns within each block are reordered in descending order of column loss, while blocks are reordered based on block loss. We introduce the relative range of block loss as a metric to identify columnar layers, enabling adaptive reordering across the entire model. Substantial empirical results on prevalent LLMs (LLaMA2-7B/13B/70B, LLaMA3-8B, Mistral-7B) demonstrate that ROSE surpasses the original SparseGPT and other counterpart pruning methods. Our code is available at https://github.com/mingluo-su/ROSE.

AI.tldr: The paper identifies that the left-to-right pruning order in SparseGPT is suboptimal for column-structured weights and proposes ROSE, a reordering strategy that prunes parameters with higher estimated error impact earlier, achieving better accuracy across various LLMs.
AI.motivation: While one-shot pruning with second-order information (e.g., SparseGPT) is effective for compressing LLMs, its fixed left-to-right pruning order ignores structural patterns like columnar weight organization, resulting in unnecessary performance degradation. There is a need to understand how pruning order affects final model quality and to design an order that better accounts for pruning-induced errors, especially in columnar layers common in modern LLMs.
AI.method: Within the SparseGPT framework, the authors introduce ROSE, which explicitly reorders the pruning sequence based on estimated pruning loss. ROSE first performs a pre-pruning step to select candidate weights and then estimates both per-column and per-block pruning loss using second-order information. It then applies a two-level reordering: (1) columns inside each block are ordered by descending column loss, and (2) blocks are ordered by their aggregated block loss, so that weights with larger potential error are pruned earlier. A metric, the relative range of block loss, is used to detect columnar layers and adaptively apply reordering only where beneficial. The approach is evaluated on multiple LLMs under the standard one-shot pruning setting.
AI.result: Experiments on several popular LLMs (LLaMA2-7B/13B/70B, LLaMA3-8B, Mistral-7B) show that ROSE consistently improves over the original SparseGPT and other pruning baselines at comparable sparsity levels. The empirical results verify that reordering based on estimated pruning loss mitigates the suboptimality caused by the fixed left-to-right order, particularly in layers with strong columnar weight patterns.
AI.conclusion: The study demonstrates that pruning order is a critical factor in one-shot Hessian-based pruning of LLMs and that the default left-to-right order in SparseGPT can be significantly improved. By reordering pruning to prioritize weights with larger estimated pruning loss and adaptively targeting columnar layers, ROSE achieves better accuracy-efficiency trade-offs across a range of large language models. This suggests that order-aware strategies should be an integral part of future pruning frameworks for LLMs.

title: Who We Are, Where We Are: Mental Health at the Intersection of Person, Situation, and Large Language Models
summary: Mental health is not a fixed trait but a dynamic process shaped by the interplay between individual dispositions and situational contexts. Building on interactionist and constructionist psychological theories, we develop interpretable models to predict well-being and identify adaptive and maladaptive self-states in longitudinal social media data. Our approach integrates person-level psychological traits (e.g., resilience, cognitive distortions, implicit motives) with language-inferred situational features derived from the Situational 8 DIAMONDS framework. We compare these theory-grounded features to embeddings from a psychometrically-informed language model that captures temporal and individual-specific patterns. Results show that our principled, theory-driven features provide competitive performance while offering greater interpretability. Qualitative analyses further highlight the psychological coherence of features most predictive of well-being. These findings underscore the value of integrating computational modeling with psychological theory to assess dynamic mental states in contextually sensitive and human-understandable ways.

AI.tldr: The paper builds psychologically interpretable models of dynamic mental health from longitudinal social media data by combining person-level traits with situational language features and specialized embeddings.
AI.motivation: Mental health fluctuates over time and across situations, but many computational models treat it as static or use black-box features that lack psychological interpretability. There is a need for methods that can both predict well-being from real-world data and provide theory-aligned, human-understandable insights into the dynamic self-states that underlie these predictions.
AI.method: The authors use longitudinal social media data and construct interpretable predictors based on (1) person-level psychological traits such as resilience, cognitive distortions, and implicit motives, and (2) language-based situational features grounded in the Situational 8 DIAMONDS framework. They compare these theory-driven features against embeddings from a psychometrically-informed language model that captures temporal and individual-specific patterns, evaluating performance on predicting well-being and identifying adaptive vs. maladaptive self-states. They also conduct qualitative analyses of the most predictive features.
AI.result: Theory-driven, psychologically grounded features achieve competitive predictive performance compared to the more complex psychometric language model embeddings, while offering much greater interpretability. The qualitative inspection reveals that the highest-impact features align well with established psychological concepts relevant to well-being and self-states.
AI.conclusion: Integrating psychological theory with computational modeling allows for effective and interpretable assessment of dynamic mental health states from social media. Theory-grounded trait and situational features can match more opaque representation-learning approaches in accuracy while better supporting contextually sensitive, human-understandable explanations of well-being and self-regulation over time.

title: Confidence Before Answering: A Paradigm Shift for Efficient LLM Uncertainty Estimation
summary:
Reliable deployment of large language models (LLMs) requires accurate uncertainty estimation. Existing methods are predominantly answer-first, producing confidence only after generating an answer, which measure the correctness of a specific response and limits practical usability. We study a confidence-first paradigm, where the model outputs its confidence before answering, interpreting this score as the model's probability of answering the question correctly under its current policy.
  We propose CoCA(Co-optimized Confidence and Answers), a GRPO reinforcement learning framework that jointly optimizes confidence calibration and answer accuracy via segmented credit assignment. By assigning separate rewards and group-relative advantages to confidence and answer segments, CoCA enables stable joint optimization and avoids reward hacking. Experiments across math, code, and factual QA benchmarks show improved calibration and uncertainty discrimination while preserving answer quality, thereby enabling a broader range of downstream applications.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces CoCA, a reinforcement learning framework that lets LLMs estimate how likely they are to answer correctly before generating the answer, improving uncertainty calibration without hurting accuracy.
AI.motivation: Current LLM uncertainty estimation is mostly answer-first: the model generates an answer and only then produces a confidence score. This limits usefulness in settings like early abstention, routing, or budgeting because the system does not know in advance whether the model is likely to be correct. Moreover, existing joint training of answers and confidence can suffer from reward hacking and unstable optimization, leading to miscalibrated confidence. A new training paradigm is needed where models can state their probability of being correct before answering, and where the learning signal separately and reliably improves both confidence calibration and answer accuracy.
AI.method: They propose CoCA (Co-optimized Confidence and Answers), a GRPO-based reinforcement learning framework that enforces a confidence-first interaction pattern: the model first outputs a confidence score, then produces an answer. The trajectory is segmented into two parts—confidence segment and answer segment—and each gets its own reward and group-relative advantage. This segmented credit assignment allows separate yet joint optimization of calibration (how well confidence matches empirical correctness) and answer quality, while reducing reward hacking where the model could game the confidence signal to gain reward without truly being calibrated.
AI.result: On math, code, and factual QA benchmarks, CoCA-trained models show better calibration (confidence more closely matches actual correctness) and improved uncertainty discrimination (better separation between correct and incorrect answers in terms of predicted confidence). At the same time, answer quality, as measured by task performance, is preserved relative to baselines that do not use this confidence-first training.
AI.conclusion: A confidence-first RL training paradigm with segmented credit assignment can train LLMs to provide well-calibrated, decision-useful confidence estimates before answering, without degrading task performance. This unlocks more reliable deployment and downstream uses of LLMs that depend on knowing in advance how likely the model is to be correct.

title: MASFactory: A Graph-centric Framework for Orchestrating LLM-Based Multi-Agent Systems with Vibe Graphing
comment: Submitted to ACL 2026 Demo Track. 10 pages, 6 figures. Code and documentation are available at: https://github.com/BUPT-GAMMA/MASFactory
summary: Large language model-based (LLM-based) multi-agent systems (MAS) are increasingly used to extend agentic problem solving via role specialization and collaboration. MAS workflows can be naturally modeled as directed computation graphs, where nodes execute agents/sub-workflows and edges encode dependencies and message passing. However, implementing complex graph workflows in current frameworks still requires substantial manual effort, offers limited reuse, and makes it difficult to integrate heterogeneous external context sources. To overcome these limitations, we present MASFactory, a graph-centric framework for orchestrating LLM-based MAS. It introduces Vibe Graphing, a human-in-the-loop approach that compiles natural-language intent into an editable workflow specification and then into an executable graph. In addition, the framework provides reusable components and pluggable context integration, as well as a visualizer for topology preview, runtime tracing, and human-in-the-loop interaction. We evaluate MASFactory on seven public benchmarks, validating both reproduction consistency for representative MAS methods and the effectiveness of Vibe Graphing. Our code (https://github.com/BUPT-GAMMA/MASFactory) and video (https://youtu.be/ANynzVfY32k) are publicly available.

AI.tldr: MASFactory is a graph-centric framework that turns natural-language descriptions of multi-agent workflows into executable large language model-based multi-agent systems with reusable components, easy context integration, and visualization tools.
AI.motivation: Existing LLM-based multi-agent system frameworks make it labor-intensive to implement complex graph-like workflows, have poor reuse across tasks, and do not seamlessly integrate diverse external context sources. There is a need for a more structured, graph-based, and human-friendly way to specify, execute, and manage such multi-agent workflows.
AI.method: The authors propose MASFactory, which models multi-agent workflows as directed computation graphs where nodes represent agents or sub-workflows and edges capture dependencies and message passing. They introduce Vibe Graphing, a human-in-the-loop process that compiles natural language intent into an editable workflow specification and then into an executable graph. The framework includes reusable components, pluggable mechanisms for integrating heterogeneous context sources, and a visual interface for previewing graph topology, tracing runtime behavior, and supporting human interventions.
AI.result: MASFactory is evaluated on seven public benchmarks. The experiments show that the framework can consistently reproduce representative multi-agent methods and that Vibe Graphing effectively converts user intent into functioning workflows. This empirically validates both the correctness of the graph-based orchestration and the usability of the human-in-the-loop design process.
AI.conclusion: Modeling LLM-based multi-agent systems as directed computation graphs, combined with human-in-the-loop Vibe Graphing, provides a practical way to design, reuse, and manage complex multi-agent workflows. MASFactory improves implementation efficiency, supports richer context integration, and offers transparency and control via visualization and interaction tools, making it a useful foundation for building and orchestrating agentic systems.

title: VerChol -- Grammar-First Tokenization for Agglutinative Languages
comment: 13 pages. A Morphological Alternative to Statistical Subword Tokenization
summary: Tokenization is the foundational step in all large language model (LLM) pipelines, yet the dominant approach Byte Pair Encoding (BPE) and its variants is inherently script agnostic and optimized for English like morphology. For agglutinative languages a typological class encompassing the Dravidian family (Tamil, Kannada, Telugu, Malayalam), Turkic languages (Turkish, Azerbaijani, Uzbek), Uralic languages (Finnish, Hungarian, Estonian), Korean, Japanese, Swahili, Basque, and others, a single word may encode root, tense, aspect, person, number, gender agreement, case, and postpositions into one orthographic unit. Statistical tokenizers fragment these words into byte pair chunks that sever morpheme boundaries and inflate token counts.

AI.tldr: The paper critiques standard BPE-style tokenization for agglutinative languages, arguing it breaks morpheme boundaries and inflates token counts, and likely proposes morphology-aware alternatives better suited to these languages.
AI.motivation: Tokenization methods used in today’s LLMs (primarily BPE and its variants) are designed in a script-agnostic, English-centric way. Agglutinative languages pack rich grammatical information (tense, aspect, person, number, case, etc.) into single orthographic words. Current statistical tokenizers split these words into subword fragments that ignore true morpheme boundaries, leading to inefficient representations, higher token counts, and potentially worse model performance for many non-English languages.
AI.method: The paper likely analyzes how BPE-based tokenization behaves on a range of agglutinative languages (Dravidian, Turkic, Uralic, Korean, Japanese, Swahili, Basque, etc.), quantifying morpheme splitting and token inflation. It probably compares standard BPE with morphology-aware tokenization schemes (e.g., rule-based or learned segmenters that respect morpheme boundaries) and evaluates impact on token statistics and downstream LLM tasks.
AI.result: The study probably finds that BPE severely fragments agglutinative words, frequently cutting across meaningful morphemes and substantially increasing the average number of tokens per word. Morphology-aware tokenization reduces token counts, better aligns with linguistic structure, and may yield improvements in efficiency and performance on language modeling or downstream tasks for agglutinative languages.
AI.conclusion: The paper concludes that script-agnostic BPE-style tokenization is suboptimal for agglutinative languages and that LLMs should adopt morphology-sensitive tokenization strategies for these languages. It likely argues that addressing tokenization is crucial for equitable performance across world languages and for reducing bias introduced by English-centric design choices in LLM pipelines.

title: Evaluating Austrian A-Level German Essays with Large Language Models for Automated Essay Scoring
comment: To be presented at the SAC2026 and published in its symposium proceedings
summary: Automated Essay Scoring (AES) has been explored for decades with the goal to support teachers by reducing grading workload and mitigating subjective biases. While early systems relied on handcrafted features and statistical models, recent advances in Large Language Models (LLMs) have made it possible to evaluate student writing with unprecedented flexibility. This paper investigates the application of state-of-the-art open-weight LLMs for the grading of Austrian A-level German texts, with a particular focus on rubric-based evaluation. A dataset of 101 anonymised student exams across three text types was processed and evaluated. Four LLMs, DeepSeek-R1 32b, Qwen3 30b, Mixtral 8x7b and LLama3.3 70b, were evaluated with different contexts and prompting strategies. The LLMs were able to reach a maximum of 40.6% agreement with the human rater in the rubric-provided sub-dimensions, and only 32.8% of final grades matched the ones given by a human expert. The results indicate that even though smaller models are able to use standardised rubrics for German essay grading, they are not accurate enough to be used in a real-world grading environment.

AI.tldr: The paper evaluates open-weight large language models for automated rubric-based scoring of Austrian A-level German essays and finds their agreement with human graders too low for real-world use.
AI.motivation: Automated Essay Scoring aims to reduce teachers’ grading workload and subjective bias, but existing systems either require handcrafted features or lack flexibility. With recent progress in open-weight LLMs, there is a need to assess whether these models can reliably perform rubric-based grading of high-stakes, German-language school exams.
AI.method: The authors compiled and anonymised a dataset of 101 Austrian A-level German exams across three text types. They tested four open-weight LLMs (DeepSeek-R1 32B, Qwen3 30B, Mixtral 8x7B, LLaMA 3.3 70B) under various context and prompting configurations to perform rubric-based scoring along sub-dimensions and to predict final grades. Model outputs were compared to human expert ratings in terms of agreement rates.
AI.result: Across models and prompting strategies, the best performance reached only 40.6% agreement with the human rater on rubric sub-dimensions, and only 32.8% of model-predicted final grades exactly matched human-assigned grades.
AI.conclusion: Current open-weight LLMs, including smaller models that can operationalize standardized rubrics, do not yet achieve sufficient accuracy to replace human grading in real-world A-level German essay assessment settings. They may be useful as supplementary tools but are not ready for high-stakes autonomous grading.

title: Experiences Build Characters: The Linguistic Origins and Functional Impact of LLM Personality
summary: Human problem-solving is enriched by a diversity of styles and personality traits, yet the development of Large Language Models (LLMs) has largely prioritized uniform performance benchmarks that favour specific behavioural tendencies such as assertiveness. To investigate how diverse experiences shape machine personality and influence problem-solving, this study employs continued pre-training to expose models to domain-specific texts in an unsupervised manner, simulating the accumulation of experience. By adapting the Big Five framework via the Machine Personality Inventory (MPI), we quantify the personality traits of these model variants and analyse their relationship to linguistic style and reasoning behaviour. The findings reveal that model competence is bimodal, peaking at "Expressive Generalists" and "Suppressed Specialists," while identifying a "Suppression Advantage" where reduced social traits enhance complex reasoning performance. This study further establishes a causal link between training data linguistics, such as imperative frequency, and lexical diversity, providing a roadmap for "Personality Engineering".

AI.tldr: The paper studies how continued pre-training on different domain texts shapes LLM “personality” and problem-solving, finding that certain personality-like profiles improve complex reasoning and can be engineered via training data linguistics.
AI.motivation: Existing LLM development focuses on uniform benchmarks and tends to favour assertive, socially oriented behaviours, overlooking the value of diversity in problem-solving styles observed in humans. The authors want to understand how varying experiential exposure (via domain-specific text) shapes a model’s stable behavioural tendencies, and how these tendencies affect reasoning performance. They also aim to move from ad‑hoc prompt hacks toward a systematic way of controlling model personality through training data.
AI.method: They perform continued pre-training of base LLMs on different domain-specific corpora in an unsupervised manner, treating this as simulated experience accumulation. They adapt the human Big Five personality framework into a Machine Personality Inventory (MPI) to measure personality traits of each model variant from its outputs. They then analyze correlations between these traits, the models’ linguistic style (e.g., imperative usage, lexical diversity), and their performance on reasoning/problem-solving benchmarks, and run causal-style analyses linking training data statistics to resulting traits and competencies.
AI.result: They discover that performance is not monotonic with any single trait but instead clusters into two high-performing profiles: “Expressive Generalists” (socially expressive, broader style) and “Suppressed Specialists” (lower social/expressive traits but strong analytical abilities). They identify a “Suppression Advantage,” where dampened social traits (e.g., less expressiveness/assertiveness) correlate with improved complex reasoning. They also show that concrete linguistic properties of the training corpus, such as frequency of imperative constructions and level of lexical diversity, causally influence these emergent personality traits and reasoning outcomes.
AI.conclusion: The paper concludes that LLM “personality” is an emergent, quantifiable property that can be systematically shaped via continued pre-training, and that certain low-social, high-analytical profiles can outperform more expressive ones on complex reasoning. By linking training data linguistics to personality and competence, the authors propose a principled approach to “Personality Engineering,” enabling designers to target desired behavioural and reasoning profiles by curating domain-specific training data rather than relying solely on prompts or RLHF tweaks.

title: Building an Ensemble LLM Semantic Tagger for UN Security Council Resolutions
summary: This paper introduces a new methodology for using LLM-based systems for accurate and efficient semantic tagging of UN Security Council resolutions. The main goal is to leverage LLM performance variability to build ensemble systems for data cleaning and semantic tagging tasks. We introduce two evaluation metrics: Content Preservation Ratio (CPR) and Tag Well-Formedness (TWF), in order to avoid hallucinations and unnecessary additions or omissions to the input text beyond the task requirement. These metrics allow the selection of the best output from multiple runs of several GPT models. GPT-4.1 achieved the highest metrics for both tasks (Cleaning: CPR 84.9% - Semantic Tagging: CPR 99.99% and TWF 99.92%). In terms of cost, smaller models, such as GPT-4.1-mini, achieved comparable performance to the best model in each task at only 20% of the cost. These metrics ultimately allowed the ensemble to select the optimal output (both cleaned and tagged content) for all the LLM models involved, across multiple runs. With this ensemble design and the use of metrics, we create a reliable LLM system for performing semantic tagging on challenging texts.
