AI.tldr: The paper proposes an LLM-based ensemble method with new evaluation metrics to reliably clean and semantically tag UN Security Council resolutions while minimizing hallucinations, and shows that both large and smaller GPT models can be effectively combined for cost-efficient, high-quality results.
AI.motivation: Semantic tagging of UN Security Council resolutions is challenging because the texts are complex and current automated approaches can introduce hallucinations, omissions, or unnecessary modifications. LLM outputs are variable across models and runs, and this variability is usually seen as a weakness. The authors want to turn this variability into an advantage by building ensembles that can automatically select the best output while strictly preserving the original content and structure required for legal and policy uses.
AI.method: The authors design an ensemble system that runs multiple GPT-family LLMs (including GPT-4.1 and GPT-4.1-mini) several times on the tasks of text cleaning and semantic tagging of resolutions. They introduce two custom intrinsic metrics: Content Preservation Ratio (CPR), measuring how faithfully the output preserves the original content without additions or deletions beyond the task; and Tag Well-Formedness (TWF), measuring the structural and syntactic correctness of the produced semantic tags. For each input, the system scores the candidate outputs from all models and runs using CPR and TWF, and then selects the best-performing output as the final result.
AI.result: GPT-4.1 produced the highest-quality outputs across tasks, with Cleaning CPR of 84.9% and near-perfect performance on Semantic Tagging (CPR 99.99%, TWF 99.92%). Smaller models such as GPT-4.1-mini achieved performance close to the best model’s scores but at roughly 20% of the cost. By combining outputs and using the CPR and TWF metrics to choose among them, the ensemble was able to consistently select high-quality cleaned and tagged versions of the resolutions from the pool of candidate outputs.
AI.conclusion: The proposed metric-driven ensemble approach transforms LLM output variability into a strength, enabling reliable and cost-effective semantic tagging for complex, high-stakes documents like UN Security Council resolutions. CPR and TWF are effective tools for detecting hallucinations and structural tagging errors and for automatically choosing the best output among multiple LLM runs. This design yields a robust system for both cleaning and semantically tagging difficult legal/policy texts while balancing accuracy and computational cost.

title: Making Implicit Premises Explicit in Logical Understanding of Enthymemes
summary: Real-world arguments in text and dialogues are normally enthymemes (i.e. some of their premises and/or claims are implicit). Natural language processing (NLP) methods for handling enthymemes can potentially identify enthymemes in text but they do not decode their underlying logic, whereas logic-based approaches for handling them assume a knowledgebase with sufficient formulae that can be used to decode them via abduction. There is therefore a lack of a systematic method for translating textual components of an enthymeme into a logical argument and generating the logical formulae required for their decoding, and thereby showing logical entailment. To address this, we propose a pipeline that integrates: (1) a large language model (LLM) to generate intermediate implicit premises based on the explicit premise and claim; (2) another LLM to translate the natural language into logical formulas; and (3) a neuro-symbolic reasoner based on a SAT solver to determine entailment. We evaluate our pipeline on two enthymeme datasets, demonstrating promising performance in selecting the correct implicit premise, as measured by precision, recall, F1-score, and accuracy.

AI.tldr: The paper presents a pipeline that combines large language models and a neuro-symbolic reasoner to reconstruct and formally evaluate enthymematic arguments by generating implicit premises, translating text to logic, and checking entailment, achieving good performance on benchmark datasets.
AI.motivation: Most real-world arguments in text and dialogue are enthymemes, meaning they omit some premises or claims. Existing NLP methods can identify such arguments but cannot recover their underlying logical structure, while logic-based methods require a rich, pre-existing knowledge base for abductive reasoning. There is no systematic way to turn textual enthymemes into full logical arguments and generate the needed logical formulae for decoding and entailment checking.
AI.method: The authors build a three-stage pipeline: (1) use a large language model to infer and generate candidate implicit premises from the given explicit premise and claim; (2) use another large language model to translate both explicit and generated natural-language components into formal logical formulas; and (3) employ a neuro-symbolic reasoner implemented via a SAT solver to determine whether the reconstructed argument logically entails the claim. They then evaluate each step on dedicated enthymeme datasets.
AI.result: On two benchmark enthymeme datasets, the pipeline shows promising performance in correctly identifying and selecting the appropriate implicit premises. Performance is quantified using standard metrics such as precision, recall, F1-score, and accuracy, and the reported results indicate that the integrated approach is effective relative to the task’s difficulty.
AI.conclusion: The study demonstrates that combining LLMs for premise generation and text-to-logic translation with a SAT-based neuro-symbolic reasoner offers a viable, systematic way to reconstruct and evaluate enthymematic arguments. This integrated approach bridges the gap between NLP-based enthymeme handling and logical, knowledge-based reasoning, enabling formal entailment checking without relying solely on pre-built knowledge bases.

title: InfoGatherer: Principled Information Seeking via Evidence Retrieval and Strategic Questioning
summary: LLMs are increasingly deployed in high-stakes domains such as medical triage and legal assistance, often as document-grounded QA systems in which a user provides a description, relevant sources are retrieved, and an LLM generates a prediction. In practice, initial user queries are often underspecified, and a single retrieval pass is insufficient for reliable decision-making, leading to incorrect and overly confident answers. While follow-up questioning can elicit missing information, existing methods typically depend on implicit, unstructured confidence signals from the LLM, making it difficult to determine what remains unknown, what information matters most, and when to stop asking questions. We propose InfoGatherer, a framework that gathers missing information from two complementary sources: retrieved domain documents and targeted follow-up questions to the user. InfoGatherer models uncertainty using Dempster-Shafer belief assignments over a structured evidential network, enabling principled fusion of incomplete and potentially contradictory evidence from both sources without prematurely collapsing to a definitive answer. Across legal and medical tasks, InfoGatherer outperforms strong baselines while requiring fewer turns. By grounding uncertainty in formal evidential theory rather than heuristic LLM signals, InfoGatherer moves towards trustworthy, interpretable decision support in domains where reliability is critical.

AI.tldr: InfoGatherer is a framework that uses evidential theory to decide what information to gather—via documents and user questions—so LLMs can make more reliable decisions in high-stakes domains.
AI.motivation: LLMs used for tasks like medical triage and legal advice often get incomplete user inputs and limited retrieval, leading to overconfident but wrong answers. Existing approaches rely on vague, heuristic confidence signals from LLMs, so they can’t clearly tell what is still uncertain, what information is most valuable to ask about next, or when they’ve gathered enough evidence to answer.
AI.method: The paper introduces InfoGatherer, which explicitly models uncertainty using Dempster-Shafer belief assignments over a structured evidential network. It fuses evidence from two sources: (1) retrieved domain documents and (2) targeted follow-up questions posed to the user. The formal evidential framework allows combining incomplete or conflicting evidence without committing early to a single answer, and it provides a principled way to decide which additional information to query and when to stop.
AI.result: On both legal and medical tasks, InfoGatherer outperforms strong baseline systems in accuracy and reliability, while also requiring fewer interaction turns with the user.
AI.conclusion: By grounding uncertainty management in Dempster-Shafer evidential theory instead of opaque LLM confidence heuristics, InfoGatherer provides more trustworthy, interpretable decision support for high-stakes, document-grounded QA applications.

title: A Causal Graph Approach to Oppositional Narrative Analysis
summary: Current methods for textual analysis rely on data annotated within predefined ontologies, often embedding human bias within black-box models. Despite achieving near-perfect performance, these approaches exploit unstructured, linear pattern recognition rather than modeling the structured interactions between entities that naturally emerge in discourse. In this work, we propose a graph-based framework for the detection, analysis, and classification of oppositional narratives and their underlying entities by representing narratives as entity-interaction graphs. Moreover, by incorporating causal estimation at the node level, our approach derives a causal representation of each contribution to the final classification by distilling the constructed sentence graph into a minimal causal subgraph. Building upon this representation, we introduce a classification pipeline that outperforms existing approaches to oppositional thinking classification task.

AI.tldr: They propose a graph-based, causal-analysis framework to better detect and classify oppositional narratives in text, moving beyond black-box, pattern-based models.
AI.motivation: Existing textual analysis methods depend on ontology-annotated data and black-box models that can be biased and mainly capture linear patterns, not the structured interactions between entities in discourse. There is a need for interpretable models that explicitly represent and reason about how entities interact, especially for complex phenomena like oppositional narratives.
AI.method: They represent narratives as entity-interaction graphs, where nodes are entities and edges capture interactions within discourse. On top of this graph, they incorporate node-level causal estimation to derive a causal representation of how each entity and interaction contributes to a final classification. This is done by distilling the original sentence graph into a minimal causal subgraph that still supports the classification decision, and then using this representation in a classification pipeline.
AI.result: The proposed graph-based, causal framework yields a classification pipeline that surpasses existing methods on the task of classifying oppositional thinking or oppositional narratives.
AI.conclusion: Graph-based representations of narratives, combined with node-level causal estimation and minimal causal subgraph extraction, provide a more effective and interpretable way to detect and classify oppositional narratives than traditional ontology-based, pattern-focused approaches.

title: Learning Next Action Predictors from Human-Computer Interaction
summary: Truly proactive AI systems must anticipate what we will do next. This foresight demands far richer information than the sparse signals we type into our prompts -- it demands reasoning over the entire context of what we see and do. We formalize this as next action prediction (NAP): given a sequence of a user's multimodal interactions with a computer (screenshots, clicks, sensor data), predict that user's next action. Progress on this task requires both new data and modeling approaches. To scale data, we annotate longitudinal, naturalistic computer use with vision-language models. We release an open-source pipeline for performing this labeling on private infrastructure, and label over 360K actions across one month of continuous phone usage from 20 users, amounting to 1,800 hours of screen time. We then introduce LongNAP, a user model that combines parametric and in-context learning to reason over long interaction histories. LongNAP is trained via policy gradient methods to generate user-specific reasoning traces given some context; retrieve relevant traces from a library of past traces; and then apply retrieved traces in-context to predict future actions. Using an LLM-as-judge evaluation metric (0-1 similarity to ground truth), LongNAP significantly outperforms supervised finetuning and prompted baselines on held-out data (by 79% and 39% respectively). Additionally, LongNAP generalizes to held out users when trained across individuals. The space of next actions a user might take at any moment is unbounded, spanning thousands of possible outcomes. Despite this, 17.1% of LongNAP's predicted trajectories are well-aligned with what a user does next (LLM-judge score $\geq$ 0.5). This rises to 26% when we filter to highly confident predictions. In sum, we argue that learning from the full context of user behavior to anticipate user needs is now a viable task with substantial opportunity.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces Next Action Prediction (NAP) for proactive AI: given a long multimodal history of a user’s interactions with their device, predict the user’s next action. They build a large, automatically labeled dataset of smartphone usage and propose LongNAP, a hybrid parametric + in-context learning model trained with policy gradients to generate and retrieve reasoning traces for better predictions, significantly outperforming baselines.
AI.motivation: Proactive AI assistants need to anticipate user actions, but current systems rely on short, sparse prompts and ignore rich contextual signals such as full-screen content, click history, and sensor data. There is no standard task, dataset, or modeling framework that uses long-horizon, multimodal interaction histories to predict what users will do next, limiting progress toward anticipatory interfaces.
AI.method: 1) Define the Next Action Prediction (NAP) task over multimodal streams of user-computer interactions (screens, clicks, sensor data). 2) Build a longitudinal dataset of smartphone usage: ~1,800 hours from 20 users, automatically labeled with >360K actions using vision-language models via an open-source private-labeling pipeline. 3) Propose LongNAP, a user model that: (a) uses policy-gradient training to learn to generate user-specific reasoning traces from context, (b) retrieves relevant traces from a library of past traces, and (c) conditions on these retrieved traces in-context to predict future actions. 4) Evaluate with an LLM-as-judge metric that scores similarity between predicted and true trajectories from 0–1, and compare to supervised finetuning and prompted baselines.
AI.result: LongNAP substantially outperforms alternatives on held-out data according to an LLM-judge similarity metric: a 79% improvement over supervised finetuning and 39% over prompted baselines. The model generalizes to unseen users when trained across individuals. Despite a huge and unbounded action space, 17.1% of predicted trajectories are judged reasonably well-aligned with actual user behavior (score ≥ 0.5), rising to 26% on high-confidence predictions.
AI.conclusion: Anticipating user actions from full-context, multimodal interaction histories is both feasible and promising. With a scalable, privacy-preserving data-labeling pipeline and the LongNAP architecture that combines parametric learning, in-context learning, and retrieval of reasoning traces, next action prediction becomes a tractable benchmark for building proactive AI assistants, with substantial headroom for future improvements.

title: CRIMSON: A Clinically-Grounded LLM-Based Metric for Generative Radiology Report Evaluation
summary: We introduce CRIMSON, a clinically grounded evaluation framework for chest X-ray report generation that assesses reports based on diagnostic correctness, contextual relevance, and patient safety. Unlike prior metrics, CRIMSON incorporates full clinical context, including patient age, indication, and guideline-based decision rules, and prevents normal or clinically insignificant findings from exerting disproportionate influence on the overall score. The framework categorizes errors into a comprehensive taxonomy covering false findings, missing findings, and eight attribute-level errors (e.g., location, severity, measurement, and diagnostic overinterpretation). Each finding is assigned a clinical significance level (urgent, actionable non-urgent, non-actionable, or expected/benign), based on a guideline developed in collaboration with attending cardiothoracic radiologists, enabling severity-aware weighting that prioritizes clinically consequential mistakes over benign discrepancies. CRIMSON is validated through strong alignment with clinically significant error counts annotated by six board-certified radiologists in ReXVal (Kendalls tau = 0.61-0.71; Pearsons r = 0.71-0.84), and through two additional benchmarks that we introduce. In RadJudge, a targeted suite of clinically challenging pass-fail scenarios, CRIMSON shows consistent agreement with expert judgment. In RadPref, a larger radiologist preference benchmark of over 100 pairwise cases with structured error categorization, severity modeling, and 1-5 overall quality ratings from three cardiothoracic radiologists, CRIMSON achieves the strongest alignment with radiologist preferences. We release the metric, the evaluation benchmarks, RadJudge and RadPref, and a fine-tuned MedGemma model to enable reproducible evaluation of report generation, all available at https://github.com/rajpurkarlab/CRIMSON.

AI.tldr: CRIMSON is a clinically grounded evaluation framework and metric for chest X-ray report generation that prioritizes diagnostic correctness and patient safety, validated against expert radiologists and released with supporting benchmarks and model.
AI.motivation: Existing automatic metrics for chest X-ray report generation often misalign with clinical needs: they over-weight trivial or benign discrepancies, under-use patient context (age, indication), and do not explicitly account for guideline-based clinical decision rules or severity of errors. Clinicians care most about safety-critical diagnostic correctness and clinically meaningful errors, not surface similarity. There is a need for an evaluation framework that reflects real radiology practice and correlates strongly with radiologist judgments of clinically significant errors and overall report quality.
AI.method: The authors design CRIMSON, an evaluation framework that compares generated chest X-ray reports against references using a clinically structured error taxonomy and full clinical context (age, indication, guidelines). They categorize errors into false findings, missing findings, and eight types of attribute-level errors (e.g., location, severity, measurement, and overinterpretation). Each finding is assigned a clinical significance class (urgent, actionable non-urgent, non-actionable, expected/benign) using a guideline co-developed with cardiothoracic radiologists. Scores are computed with severity-aware weighting so that errors with higher clinical impact affect the metric more. They validate CRIMSON by correlating its scores and error counts with annotations from six board-certified radiologists on the ReXVal dataset, and by testing on two new benchmarks: RadJudge, a set of challenging pass/fail clinical cases, and RadPref, a radiologist preference benchmark with pairwise comparisons and quality ratings. They also release an associated fine-tuned MedGemma model for report generation.
AI.result: CRIMSON demonstrates strong correlation with radiologist-assessed clinically significant error counts on ReXVal (Kendall's tau 0.61–0.71, Pearson's r 0.71–0.84). On the RadJudge benchmark of challenging clinical scenarios, its pass/fail decisions consistently match expert judgments. On the larger RadPref benchmark with over 100 pairwise comparisons and structured preference data from three cardiothoracic radiologists, CRIMSON shows the highest alignment with radiologist preferences among evaluated metrics. The authors also provide open-source implementations of CRIMSON, the RadJudge and RadPref benchmarks, and a fine-tuned MedGemma model for reproducible evaluation and research.
AI.conclusion: CRIMSON offers a clinically grounded, safety-aware metric for chest X-ray report generation that better reflects radiology practice than prior text-based metrics. By explicitly modeling clinical context, a detailed error taxonomy, and severity-aware weighting based on radiologist-developed guidelines, it prioritizes clinically consequential mistakes over benign differences. Its strong empirical alignment with radiologist error annotations and preferences, along with the release of associated benchmarks and a fine-tuned model, positions CRIMSON as a robust standard for evaluating and advancing automated chest X-ray reporting systems.

title: MAPO: Mixed Advantage Policy Optimization for Long-Horizon Multi-Turn Dialogue
summary: Subjective multi-turn dialogue tasks, such as emotional support, require conversational policies that adapt to evolving user states and optimize long-horizon interaction quality. However, reinforcement learning (RL) for such settings remains challenging due to the absence of reliable process supervision. Outcome-only training collapses credit assignment across turns into a single trajectory-level reward, while naïve turn-level group sampling incurs prohibitive rollout costs in interactive environments. We propose a critic-free and efficient RL algorithm named MAPO that leverages dense process feedback from a judge model and propagates long-horizon effects through Monte Carlo returns. To stabilize optimization, we introduce a mixed advantage estimator that combines turn-level normalization with batch-level normalization, enabling fine-grained yet scalable credit assignment. Across multiple subjective dialogue benchmarks, including EMPA, EmoBench, and EQ-Bench, and model scales ranging from 7B to 32B, our method consistently improves both training stability and final performance over outcome-only GRPO and single-level normalization baselines. On EMPA, we improve rates by up to 9 points and increase dialogue scores by as much as +43.2 over the 7B base model. Despite training only on EMPA-style environments, our approach generalizes well, yielding consistent improvements on unseen emotional-intelligence benchmarks, including up to +4 points on EmoBench and +3.5 on EQ-Bench. Together, these results demonstrate that dense process supervision combined with mixed-level normalization enables effective and scalable RL for subjective, open-ended multi-turn dialogue.

AI.tldr: They propose MAPO, a critic-free RL algorithm using dense process feedback and mixed-level normalization to improve subjective multi-turn dialogue systems, achieving better stability, credit assignment, and performance across several emotional dialogue benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Subjective, multi-turn dialogue tasks (e.g., emotional support) need policies that adapt over long interactions, but RL is hard here because there’s no reliable turn-by-turn supervision. Existing outcome-only RL loses fine-grained credit assignment across turns, and naïve methods that try to assign turn-level rewards are too computationally expensive. The authors aim to design an RL method that can use rich process feedback efficiently while handling long-horizon credit assignment in subjective dialogue settings.
AI.method: They introduce MAPO, a critic-free RL algorithm that uses a judge model to provide dense process feedback (turn-level evaluations) and then aggregates these via Monte Carlo returns to capture long-horizon effects. To make optimization stable and scalable, they design a mixed advantage estimator that blends turn-level normalization (for fine-grained credit) with batch-level normalization (for global stability), enabling efficient credit assignment over multi-turn conversations without expensive rollout schemes or learned critics.
AI.result: On multiple subjective dialogue benchmarks (EMPA, EmoBench, EQ-Bench) and across models from 7B to 32B parameters, MAPO improves training stability and final performance relative to outcome-only GRPO and baselines that use only a single normalization level. On EMPA, it yields up to a 9-point improvement in success rates and boosts dialogue quality scores by up to +43.2 over the 7B base model; it also transfers well, giving up to +4 points on EmoBench and +3.5 on EQ-Bench despite training only on EMPA-style environments.
AI.conclusion: Dense, judge-provided process supervision combined with their mixed-level advantage normalization makes critic-free, scalable RL feasible for subjective, open-ended, multi-turn dialogue. MAPO effectively addresses long-horizon credit assignment and stability challenges and generalizes beyond the training environment to other emotional-intelligence benchmarks.

title: Implicit Style Conditioning: A Structured Style-Rewrite Framework for Low-Resource Character Modeling
summary: Large Language Models (LLMs) have demonstrated impressive capabilities in role-playing (RP); however, small Language Models (SLMs) with highly stylized personas remains a challenge due to data scarcity and the complexity of style disentanglement. Standard Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT) often captures surface-level semantics while failing to reproduce the intricate syntactic and pragmatic nuances of a character, leading to "Out-Of-Character" (OOC) generation. To address this, we propose a Structured Style-Rewrite Framework that explicitly disentangles style into three interpretable dimensions: lexical signatures (via PMI), syntactic patterns (grounded in PCFG rules), and pragmatic style. Furthermore, we introduce an implicit style conditioning strategy via Chain-of-Thought (CoT) distillation. By leveraging explicit reasoning traces during training as a strong inductive bias, our approach aligns the model's latent representations with structured style features, enabling high-fidelity stylized generation without requiring explicit reasoning tokens during inference. Extensive experiments on a specific high-stylization domain (anime characters) demonstrate that our method enables a Qwen-1.7B model to outperform significantly larger baselines (e.g., 4B Vanilla SFT) in style consistency and semantic fidelity. Our approach offers a data-efficient paradigm for democratizing inference and deployment on consumer hardware.

AI.tldr: A framework for training small language models to role-play highly stylized characters by explicitly modeling and distilling style, achieving better in-character generation than larger standard SFT models.
AI.motivation: Small language models struggle to faithfully role-play characters with rich, distinctive styles (e.g., anime characters) because stylized data are scarce and standard supervised fine-tuning mainly captures surface semantics, causing out-of-character outputs. There is a need for a data-efficient method that can disentangle and learn complex stylistic properties so SLMs can match or exceed larger models while being cheap to deploy.
AI.method: They propose a Structured Style-Rewrite Framework that decomposes style into three explicit, interpretable components: (1) lexical signatures identified via pointwise mutual information (PMI) to capture distinctive word choices; (2) syntactic patterns grounded in probabilistic context-free grammar (PCFG) rules to capture sentence structure; and (3) pragmatic style to capture discourse-level and interactional behavior. In addition, they use Chain-of-Thought distillation as an implicit style conditioning strategy: explicit reasoning traces are included during training to bias the latent representations toward these structured style features, but at inference the model generates without CoT tokens, having internalized the style implicitly.
AI.result: On a high-stylization benchmark focused on anime character role-play, their method allows a relatively small Qwen-1.7B model to surpass significantly larger models trained with vanilla supervised fine-tuning (e.g., 4B SFT) in both style consistency (staying in character) and semantic fidelity (correctly preserving intended meaning and content).
AI.conclusion: Explicitly structuring and disentangling stylistic features, combined with CoT-based implicit style conditioning, enables small language models to perform high-fidelity stylized role-play more effectively and data-efficiently than larger vanilla SFT models. This offers a practical path to democratize high-quality RP on consumer hardware due to lower inference and deployment costs.

title: FlashPrefill: Instantaneous Pattern Discovery and Thresholding for Ultra-Fast Long-Context Prefilling
summary: Long-context modeling is a pivotal capability for Large Language Models, yet the quadratic complexity of attention remains a critical bottleneck, particularly during the compute-intensive prefilling phase. While various sparse attention mechanisms have been explored, they typically suffer from either significant search latency or insufficient sparsity. In this paper, we propose FlashPrefill, a framework enabling ultra-fast prefilling via instantaneous pattern discovery and thresholding. FlashPrefill leverages a fast block-searching technique to simultaneously locate dynamic vertical, slash, and block-sparse attention patterns. Crucially, it introduces a dynamic thresholding mechanism that bypasses the prohibitive overhead of sorting or accumulating attention scores while effectively eliminating the long-tail distribution to enhance sparsity. Extensive evaluations demonstrate that FlashPrefill achieves a substantial leap in efficiency, delivering an unprecedented 27.78x speedup on 256K sequences. Notably, unlike existing methods that incur efficiency degradation on shorter contexts, FlashPrefill maintains a 1.71x speedup even at a 4K context length, demonstrating its robustness and practical utility across varying sequence scales.

AI.tldr: FlashPrefill is a framework that greatly speeds up the prefilling phase of long-context LLMs by discovering sparse attention patterns and applying dynamic thresholding without expensive sorting.
AI.motivation: Long-context LLMs are bottlenecked by the quadratic cost of attention, especially in the prefilling phase when processing very long input sequences. Existing sparse attention methods either introduce large search latency or are not sparse enough to give big speedups. There is a need for a method that can automatically find effective sparsity patterns quickly and work well across both long and short contexts.
AI.method: The paper proposes FlashPrefill, which uses a fast block-searching technique to discover multiple types of sparse attention patterns (vertical, slash, and block-sparse) on the fly. It then applies a dynamic thresholding mechanism to prune attention connections based on their scores, but without using costly operations like sorting or accumulation. This thresholding is designed to remove the long-tail of small attention scores, increasing sparsity while keeping computation low.
AI.result: Experiments show that FlashPrefill provides up to 27.78x speedup for prefilling on 256K-token sequences. Importantly, unlike many sparse-attention methods that only help at very long contexts, FlashPrefill still achieves 1.71x speedup even at a 4K context length, indicating consistent efficiency gains across sequence lengths.
AI.conclusion: FlashPrefill offers a practical and efficient solution for accelerating the prefilling phase of long-context LLMs by combining fast pattern discovery with dynamic thresholding to induce high attention sparsity without heavy overhead. Its strong speedups at both long and relatively short contexts suggest it could be a robust building block for scalable LLM deployment on long sequences.

title: Abductive Reasoning with Syllogistic Forms in Large Language Models
comment: Published in Proceedings of the 3rd International Conference on Human and Artificial Rationalities (HAR 2024), LNCS 15504, pp. 3-17
summary: Research in AI using Large-Language Models (LLMs) is rapidly evolving, and the comparison of their performance with human reasoning has become a key concern. Prior studies have indicated that LLMs and humans share similar biases, such as dismissing logically valid inferences that contradict common beliefs. However, criticizing LLMs for these biases might be unfair, considering our reasoning not only involves formal deduction but also abduction, which draws tentative conclusions from limited information. Abduction can be regarded as the inverse form of syllogism in its basic structure, that is, a process of drawing a minor premise from a major premise and conclusion. This paper explores the accuracy of LLMs in abductive reasoning by converting a syllogistic dataset into one suitable for abduction. It aims to investigate whether the state-of-the-art LLMs exhibit biases in abduction and to identify potential areas for improvement, emphasizing the importance of contextualized reasoning beyond formal deduction. This investigation is vital for advancing the understanding and application of LLMs in complex reasoning tasks, offering insights into bridging the gap between machine and human cognition.

AI.tldr: The paper examines how well large language models perform abductive reasoning by transforming a syllogistic reasoning dataset into an abductive one, comparing model behavior and biases with humans, and highlighting where LLMs need improvement for contextual, non-deductive reasoning.
AI.motivation: As LLMs become central in AI research and applications, it is increasingly important to understand not only their deductive reasoning abilities but also their performance in more human-like forms of reasoning such as abduction. Prior work found that LLMs share certain reasoning biases with humans, but criticism may be unfair because humans also rely on non-deductive reasoning. This motivates a closer study of abductive reasoning in LLMs to see whether and how they are biased, and how to improve them for complex, real-world reasoning tasks.
AI.method: The authors convert an existing syllogistic dataset—originally designed for deductive reasoning—into a dataset suitable for abduction, effectively reversing the structure of syllogisms so that the task is to infer a plausible minor premise from a known major premise and conclusion. They then evaluate state-of-the-art LLMs on this abductive dataset, analyzing their performance and any systematic biases that emerge during abductive reasoning.
AI.result: The study finds that LLMs show measurable accuracy in abductive reasoning on the transformed syllogistic dataset but also exhibit systematic biases, some of which are analogous to human reasoning biases. These biases appear when models must reconcile formal logical structure with common-sense or belief-based expectations, revealing limitations in their contextual reasoning capabilities.
AI.conclusion: LLMs are capable of performing abductive reasoning to a certain extent but are prone to biases similar to those found in humans, especially when reasoning must go beyond strict formal deduction. The findings underscore the need for improved training or architectures that better support contextualized abductive reasoning. This work contributes to closing the gap between machine and human cognition in complex reasoning tasks by clarifying where current LLMs fall short and suggesting directions for enhancement.

title: Track-SQL: Enhancing Generative Language Models with Dual-Extractive Modules for Schema and Context Tracking in Multi-turn Text-to-SQL
comment: Accepted at the 2025 Conference of the Nations of the Americas Chapter of the Association for Computational Linguistics (NAACL 2025), Long Paper, 19 pages
summary: Generative language models have shown significant potential in single-turn Text-to-SQL. However, their performance does not extend equivalently to multi-turn Text-to-SQL. This is primarily due to generative language models' inadequacy in handling the complexities of context information and dynamic schema linking in multi-turn interactions. In this paper, we propose a framework named Track-SQL, which enhances generative language models with dual-extractive modules designed to track schema and contextual changes in multi-turn Text-to-SQL. Specifically, Track-SQL incorporates a \emph{Semantic-enhanced Schema Extractor} and a \emph{Schema-aware Context Extractor}. Experimental results demonstrate that Track-SQL achieves state-of-the-art performance on the SparC and CoSQL datasets. Furthermore, detailed ablation studies reveal that Track-SQL significantly improves execution accuracy in multi-turn interactions by 7.1\% and 9.55\% on these datasets, respectively. Our implementation will be open-sourced at https://github.com/DMIRLAB-Group/Track-SQL.

AI.tldr: Track-SQL is a framework that augments generative language models with dual-extractive modules to better handle context and schema changes, achieving state-of-the-art multi-turn Text-to-SQL performance on SparC and CoSQL.
AI.motivation: Existing generative language models perform well on single-turn Text-to-SQL but struggle in multi-turn settings because they cannot robustly track evolving conversation context or dynamically link to changing database schemas, leading to poor execution accuracy across turns.
AI.method: Introduce Track-SQL, which wraps a generative language model with two extractive components: (1) a Semantic-enhanced Schema Extractor that identifies and tracks relevant schema elements using semantic information; and (2) a Schema-aware Context Extractor that selects and structures prior conversational context guided by schema signals, both feeding focused information into the base generator for each turn.
AI.result: On the SparC and CoSQL multi-turn Text-to-SQL benchmarks, Track-SQL achieves new state-of-the-art performance, improving execution accuracy by 7.1% on SparC and 9.55% on CoSQL compared to prior methods; ablation studies confirm the contribution of the dual extractive modules.
AI.conclusion: By explicitly tracking schema and contextual changes through dual-extractive modules, Track-SQL overcomes key weaknesses of standard generative language models in multi-turn Text-to-SQL, yielding substantial accuracy gains and providing a practical, open-source solution for conversational database querying.

title: PONTE: Personalized Orchestration for Natural Language Trustworthy Explanations
summary: Explainable Artificial Intelligence (XAI) seeks to enhance the transparency and accountability of machine learning systems, yet most methods follow a one-size-fits-all paradigm that neglects user differences in expertise, goals, and cognitive needs. Although Large Language Models can translate technical explanations into natural language, they introduce challenges related to faithfulness and hallucinations. To address these challenges, we present PONTE (Personalized Orchestration for Natural language Trustworthy Explanations), a human-in-the-loop framework for adaptive and reliable XAI narratives. PONTE models personalization as a closed-loop validation and adaptation process rather than prompt engineering. It combines: (i) a low-dimensional preference model capturing stylistic requirements; (ii) a preference-conditioned generator grounded in structured XAI artifacts; and (iii) verification modules enforcing numerical faithfulness, informational completeness, and stylistic alignment, optionally supported by retrieval-grounded argumentation. User feedback iteratively updates the preference state, enabling quick personalization. Automatic and human evaluations across healthcare and finance domains show that the verification-refinement loop substantially improves completeness and stylistic alignment over validation-free generation. Human studies further confirm strong agreement between intended preference vectors and perceived style, robustness to generation stochasticity, and consistently positive quality assessments.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces PONTE, a human-in-the-loop framework that generates personalized, trustworthy natural language explanations for AI decisions by grounding them in XAI artifacts and validating them for faithfulness, completeness, and style.
AI.motivation: Most current explainable AI methods ignore differences between users’ expertise, goals, and cognitive preferences, resulting in generic explanations that may be unhelpful or misleading. Large Language Models can help by translating technical XAI outputs into natural language, but they risk hallucinations and unfaithful explanations. There is a need for a systematic way to generate adaptive, user-tailored explanations that remain grounded in the underlying model behavior and can be iteratively refined with user feedback.
AI.method: The authors propose PONTE, a framework that treats personalization as a closed-loop validation and adaptation process. PONTE includes: (i) a low-dimensional user preference model that encodes stylistic needs (e.g., level of detail, tone, structure); (ii) a generator that conditions on these preferences while being grounded in structured XAI artifacts (such as feature attributions or rule sets); and (iii) verification modules that automatically check numerical faithfulness to the XAI artifacts, informational completeness, and stylistic alignment. These verifiers can use retrieval-grounded argumentation to justify or refine explanations. User feedback is used to update the preference vector over iterations, gradually improving personalization.
AI.result: Through automatic and human evaluation in healthcare and finance tasks, PONTE’s verification-refinement loop significantly increases the completeness and stylistic conformity of explanations when compared with simple LLM-based generation without validation. Human subjects show high agreement between the target preference vectors and their perception of the generated style, the system remains robust to randomness in generation, and participants consistently rate the explanations as high quality.
AI.conclusion: PONTE demonstrates that framing personalized explanation generation as a closed-loop, human-in-the-loop process with explicit preference modeling and verification improves both the reliability and usefulness of XAI narratives. By grounding LLM-generated explanations in structured XAI artifacts and systematically validating them, the framework mitigates hallucination and faithfulness issues while rapidly adapting to individual user needs.

title: ViewFusion: Structured Spatial Thinking Chains for Multi-View Reasoning
summary: Multi-view spatial reasoning remains difficult for current vision-language models. Even when multiple viewpoints are available, models often underutilize cross-view relations and instead rely on single-image shortcuts, leading to fragile performance on viewpoint transformation and occlusion-sensitive cases. We present ViewFusion, a two-stage framework that explicitly separates cross-view spatial pre-alignment from question answering. In the first stage, the model performs deliberate spatial pre-thinking to infer viewpoint relations and spatial transformations across views, forming an intermediate workspace that goes beyond a simple re-description. In the second stage, the model conducts question-driven reasoning conditioned on this workspace to produce the final prediction. We train ViewFusion with synthetic reasoning supervision followed by reinforcement learning using GRPO, which improves answer correctness while stabilizing the intended two-stage generation behavior. On MMSI-Bench, ViewFusion improves accuracy by 5.3\% over Qwen3-VL-4B-Instruct, with the largest gains on examples that require genuine cross-view alignment.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces ViewFusion, a two-stage framework that improves multi-view spatial reasoning for vision-language models by explicitly pre-aligning views before answering questions, achieving better performance on cross-view tasks.
AI.motivation: Current vision-language models struggle with multi-view spatial reasoning: they underuse cross-view relations, rely on single-image shortcuts, and thus fail on viewpoint changes and occlusion-heavy scenarios. The authors aim to create a method that genuinely reasons over multiple views instead of exploiting shortcuts.
AI.method: ViewFusion uses a two-stage pipeline. Stage 1 performs explicit spatial pre-alignment ("spatial pre-thinking") to infer viewpoint relations and spatial transformations across the available views, producing an intermediate workspace representation that encodes cross-view spatial structure. Stage 2 takes this workspace plus the question and performs question-driven reasoning to output the final answer. Training combines synthetic reasoning supervision to teach the two-stage behavior with reinforcement learning via GRPO to improve answer accuracy and stabilize adherence to the two-stage generation pattern.
AI.result: On the MMSI-Bench benchmark, ViewFusion outperforms the baseline Qwen3-VL-4B-Instruct model by 5.3 percentage points in accuracy, with the largest improvements on problems that genuinely require cross-view spatial alignment instead of single-view shortcuts.
AI.conclusion: Explicitly decoupling cross-view spatial pre-alignment from question answering and training this process with synthetic reasoning supervision plus GRPO leads to more robust multi-view spatial reasoning in vision-language models, particularly for tasks that demand accurate viewpoint transformations and handling of occlusions.

title: Wisdom of the AI Crowd (AI-CROWD) for Ground Truth Approximation in Content Analysis: A Research Protocol & Validation Using Eleven Large Language Models
summary: Large-scale content analysis is increasingly limited by the absence of observable ground truth or gold-standard labels, as creating such benchmarks through extensive human coding becomes impractical for massive datasets due to high time, cost, and consistency challenges. To overcome this barrier, we introduce the AI-CROWD protocol, which approximates ground truth by leveraging the collective outputs of an ensemble of large language models (LLMs). Rather than asserting that the resulting labels are true ground truth, the protocol generates a consensus-based approximation derived from convergent and divergent inferences across multiple models. By aggregating outputs via majority voting and interrogating agreement/disagreement patterns with diagnostic metrics, AI-CROWD identifies high-confidence classifications while flagging potential ambiguity or model-specific biases.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes AI-CROWD, a protocol that uses ensembles of large language models (LLMs) to generate consensus labels as a practical approximation to ground truth for large-scale content analysis.
AI.motivation: Traditional large-scale content analysis requires gold-standard labels produced by human coders, but for massive datasets this is increasingly infeasible due to high time and financial costs, as well as difficulties ensuring coding consistency and quality. Without observable ground truth, researchers lack reliable benchmarks for training, validating, and evaluating automated methods. The paper is motivated by the need for a scalable, systematic alternative to human-generated ground truth that can support empirical research at scale while acknowledging uncertainty.
AI.method: The AI-CROWD protocol leverages an ensemble of LLMs to approximate ground truth labels. Multiple models are prompted to label the same content. Their outputs are then aggregated using majority voting to produce consensus labels. In addition to simple aggregation, the protocol analyzes convergent and divergent responses across models using diagnostic agreement metrics. This allows the identification of items where there is strong cross-model consensus (high-confidence labels) versus items where disagreement suggests ambiguity, label instability, or model-specific biases. The approach thus combines ensemble labeling with explicit uncertainty and bias diagnostics.
AI.result: The protocol produces consensus-based labels that approximate ground truth without claiming to be definitive. It yields two key outputs: (1) high-confidence classifications where multiple models agree, usable as training or evaluation data; and (2) flagged instances where model disagreement reveals ambiguous content or systematic biases. The method thereby generates large-scale labeled datasets along with interpretable indicators of label reliability. (Specific quantitative results are not provided in the abstract.)
AI.conclusion: AI-CROWD offers a practical and scalable way to approximate ground truth for large-scale content analysis by using ensembles of LLMs. While it does not replace human-labeled gold standards, it provides consensus-derived labels augmented with diagnostics about confidence, ambiguity, and bias. This enables researchers to conduct content analysis at scales that would be infeasible with manual coding alone, while still explicitly representing and managing uncertainty in the labeling process.

title: LIT-RAGBench: Benchmarking Generator Capabilities of Large Language Models in Retrieval-Augmented Generation
comment: Published as a conference paper at LREC 2026
summary: Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) is a framework in which a Generator, such as a Large Language Model (LLM), produces answers by retrieving documents from an external collection using a Retriever. In practice, Generators must integrate evidence from long contexts, perform multi-step reasoning, interpret tables, and abstain when evidence is missing. However, existing benchmarks for Generators provide limited coverage, with none enabling simultaneous evaluation of multiple capabilities under unified conditions. To bridge the gap between existing evaluations and practical use, we introduce LIT-RAGBench (the Logic, Integration, Table, Reasoning, and Abstention RAG Generator Benchmark), which defines five categories: Integration, Reasoning, Logic, Table, and Abstention, each further divided into practical evaluation aspects. LIT-RAGBench systematically covers patterns combining multiple aspects across categories. By using fictional entities and scenarios, LIT-RAGBench evaluates answers grounded in the provided external documents. The dataset consists of 114 human-constructed Japanese questions and an English version generated by machine translation with human curation. We use LLM-as-a-Judge for scoring and report category-wise and overall accuracy. Across API-based and open-weight models, no model exceeds 90% overall accuracy. By making strengths and weaknesses measurable within each category, LIT-RAGBench serves as a valuable metric for model selection in practical RAG deployments and for building RAG-specialized models. We release LIT-RAGBench, including the dataset and evaluation code, at https://github.com/Koki-Itai/LIT-RAGBench.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces LIT-RAGBench, a benchmark to comprehensively evaluate Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) generators across multiple practical capabilities, using carefully constructed Japanese questions and their English translations.
AI.motivation: Existing RAG generator benchmarks test only a limited set of capabilities and do not evaluate multiple crucial abilities—such as long-context integration, multi-step reasoning, table interpretation, and abstention—in a unified, realistic setting. This gap makes it hard to understand generator weaknesses and to select or design models suited for real-world RAG deployments.
AI.method: The authors design LIT-RAGBench, a benchmark organized into five main categories: Integration, Reasoning, Logic, Table, and Abstention. Each category is further divided into practical evaluation aspects, and the benchmark systematically includes question patterns that combine multiple aspects across categories. They construct 114 fictional, document-grounded Japanese questions and generate an English version via machine translation followed by human curation. For evaluation, they use an LLM-as-a-Judge setup to score model outputs and compute category-wise and overall accuracies across both API-based and open-weight LLMs.
AI.result: When tested on LIT-RAGBench, none of the evaluated models—whether API-based or open-weight—achieved more than 90% overall accuracy. The category-wise scores reveal distinct strengths and weaknesses across the different capabilities (Integration, Reasoning, Logic, Table, Abstention), indicating that current RAG generators are not yet reliably strong across all practically important aspects.
AI.conclusion: LIT-RAGBench provides a comprehensive, structured benchmark for evaluating RAG generators along multiple, practically relevant dimensions under unified conditions. By using fictional, fully document-grounded scenarios and LLM-as-a-Judge scoring, it enables fine-grained analysis of model strengths and weaknesses and serves both as a tool for model selection in real-world RAG systems and as a target for developing RAG-specialized models. The dataset and evaluation code are publicly released for the community.

title: SPOT: Span-level Pause-of-Thought for Efficient and Interpretable Latent Reasoning in Large Language Models
summary: Explicit Chain-of-Thought improves the reasoning performance of large language models but often incurs high inference cost due to verbose token-level traces. While recent approaches reduce this overhead via concise prompting or step pruning, they largely truncate what the model says rather than internalize what the model thinks. Latent reasoning offers a promising alternative by performing computation in the hidden space, yet prior methods face two critical challenges. Many existing approaches rely on rigid point-to-point alignment, forcing a latent token to approximate the final representation of a reasoning step, which can be insufficient to capture the dense, variable-length semantics of an entire reasoning segment. Furthermore, these methods often suffer from a lack of interpretability: latent states are commonly produced by unconstrained optimization or embedding mixing, yielding vectors that are difficult to decode or audit under the pretrained language head. We propose SPOT, a flexible framework that compresses explicit CoT into compact latent pause tokens without enforcing a fixed response template. At the core of SPOT is Span-level Semantic Alignment, a Sinkhorn optimal-transport objective that softly matches each pause token to the semantics of an entire reasoning segment, overcoming the rigidity of step-end alignment. To further improve interpretability, SPOT introduces a Frozen-Head Decoding Constraint that keeps latent states directly decodable as token distributions under the frozen pretrained LM head, enabling readable keyword interpretations of latent thoughts. Experiments on reasoning benchmarks demonstrate that SPOT improves accuracy by 2.3 points on average while reducing generated tokens by 37.5% and provides faithful semantic interpretations of the latent reasoning process.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes SPOT, a framework that compresses explicit chain-of-thought reasoning into compact latent pause tokens to speed up inference while maintaining or improving reasoning accuracy and interpretability.
AI.motivation: Explicit Chain-of-Thought reasoning boosts large language model performance but is costly because it generates many intermediate tokens. Existing compression methods mostly shorten the visible trace instead of changing how the model reasons internally, and latent reasoning methods either impose rigid alignment between latent tokens and steps or become hard to interpret. There is a need for a method that both efficiently internalizes reasoning in latent space and keeps it semantically aligned and interpretable.
AI.method: The authors introduce SPOT, which turns explicit chain-of-thought into latent pause tokens. It uses Span-level Semantic Alignment, based on a Sinkhorn optimal-transport objective, to softly align each pause token with the semantics of an entire reasoning segment instead of just its endpoint. They also add a Frozen-Head Decoding Constraint so that the latent pause states remain directly decodable through the original pretrained language model head, enabling them to be interpreted as meaningful token distributions (e.g., keywords) without changing the head.
AI.result: On reasoning benchmarks, SPOT increases accuracy by an average of 2.3 percentage points compared with baselines while cutting the number of generated tokens by 37.5%. The latent states yield readable, faithful semantic interpretations of the model’s internal reasoning, as assessed under the frozen language head.
AI.conclusion: SPOT offers an efficient and interpretable latent reasoning framework that compresses explicit chain-of-thought into compact pause tokens. By aligning these tokens with entire reasoning spans and constraining them to remain decodable, SPOT achieves better accuracy with fewer tokens and provides transparent views into the model’s internal reasoning process.

title: Mind the Gap: Pitfalls of LLM Alignment with Asian Public Opinion
summary: Large Language Models (LLMs) are increasingly being deployed in multilingual, multicultural settings, yet their reliance on predominantly English-centric training data risks misalignment with the diverse cultural values of different societies. In this paper, we present a comprehensive, multilingual audit of the cultural alignment of contemporary LLMs including GPT-4o-Mini, Gemini-2.5-Flash, Llama 3.2, Mistral and Gemma 3 across India, East Asia and Southeast Asia. Our study specifically focuses on the sensitive domain of religion as the prism for broader alignment. To facilitate this, we conduct a multi-faceted analysis of every LLM's internal representations, using log-probs/logits, to compare the model's opinion distributions against ground-truth public attitudes. We find that while the popular models generally align with public opinion on broad social issues, they consistently fail to accurately represent religious viewpoints, especially those of minority groups, often amplifying negative stereotypes. Lightweight interventions, such as demographic priming and native language prompting, partially mitigate but do not eliminate these cultural gaps. We further show that downstream evaluations on bias benchmarks (such as CrowS-Pairs, IndiBias, ThaiCLI, KoBBQ) reveal persistent harms and under-representation in sensitive contexts. Our findings underscore the urgent need for systematic, regionally grounded audits to ensure equitable global deployment of LLMs.

AI.tldr: The paper audits how well major LLMs reflect public cultural and religious attitudes across several Asian regions, finding good alignment on broad social issues but systematic misalignment and stereotype amplification around religion, especially for minority groups.
AI.motivation: As LLMs are deployed globally, they are mostly trained on English-centric data that may not reflect diverse cultural and religious values, risking biased or harmful behavior, particularly for underrepresented communities. There is a need to rigorously evaluate whether these models align with real public attitudes in different regions and cultures.
AI.method: The authors perform a multilingual audit of several contemporary LLMs (GPT-4o-Mini, Gemini-2.5-Flash, Llama 3.2, Mistral, Gemma 3) across India, East Asia, and Southeast Asia, focusing on religion as a sensitive test domain. They analyze internal model behavior through log-probabilities/logits to derive opinion distributions and compare these with ground-truth public attitude data. They also test lightweight mitigation strategies such as demographic priming and native-language prompting, and evaluate downstream performance on bias benchmarks like CrowS-Pairs, IndiBias, ThaiCLI, and KoBBQ.
AI.result: The models broadly match public opinion on general social issues but fail to faithfully represent religious perspectives, especially for minority religious groups, where they often amplify negative stereotypes. Demographic priming and native language prompting reduce but do not remove these misalignments. Benchmark evaluations confirm ongoing harms, bias, and under-representation in sensitive contexts across the tested regions.
AI.conclusion: Current LLMs, though somewhat aligned with public attitudes on general topics, are systematically misaligned on religious and minority-group perspectives, leading to stereotype amplification and inequitable treatment. Simple prompt-based fixes are insufficient, highlighting the urgent need for systematic, region-specific audits and more culturally grounded development practices to support fair global deployment of LLMs.

title: The Art That Poses Back: Assessing AI Pastiches after Contemporary Artworks
summary: This study explores artificial visual creativity, focusing on ChatGPT's ability to generate new images intentionally pastiching original artworks such as paintings, drawings, sculptures and installations. The process involved twelve artists from Romania, Bulgaria, France, Austria, and the United Kingdom, each invited to contribute with three of their artworks and to grade and comment on the AI-generated versions. The analysis combines human evaluation with computational methods aimed at detecting visual and stylistic similarities or divergences between the original works and their AI-produced renditions. The results point to a significant gap between color and texture-based similarity and compositional, conceptual, and perceptual one. Consequently, we advocate for the use of a "style transfer dashboard" of complementary metrics to evaluate the similarity between pastiches and originals, rather than using a single style metric. The artists' comments revealed limitations of ChatGPT's pastiches after contemporary artworks, which were perceived by the authors of the originals as lacking dimensionality, context, and intentional sense, and seeming more of a paraphrase or an approximate quotation rather than as a valuable, emotion-evoking artwork.

AI.tldr: Study evaluates ChatGPT’s capacity to generate intentional pastiches of contemporary artworks and proposes better metrics for assessing stylistic similarity.
AI.motivation: To understand how well current AI (specifically ChatGPT’s image generation) can intentionally imitate the style of living artists and to identify appropriate ways to measure visual and stylistic similarity beyond superficial features like color and texture.
AI.method: Twelve professional artists from several European countries submitted three artworks each. ChatGPT was prompted to generate image pastiches of these works. The AI outputs were then evaluated in two ways: (1) human evaluation, where the original artists graded and commented on the AI-generated images; and (2) computational analysis, using multiple metrics to compare visual and stylistic similarity between original and AI-generated images, with a focus on distinguishing color/texture similarity from compositional and conceptual aspects.
AI.result: The study found a major discrepancy between low-level similarity (color and texture) and higher-level similarity (composition, concept, and perception). ChatGPT’s pastiches often matched surface-level features but failed to reproduce deeper stylistic structures. This mismatch shows that relying on a single “style similarity” metric is inadequate for evaluating AI-generated pastiches.
AI.conclusion: The authors recommend a multi-dimensional “style transfer dashboard” of complementary metrics to assess similarity between AI pastiches and original artworks. They conclude that current ChatGPT image pastiches of contemporary artworks lack depth, context, intentionality, and emotional impact, leading artists to view them as paraphrases or approximate quotations rather than meaningful new artworks.

title: Transparent AI for Mathematics: Transformer-Based Large Language Models for Mathematical Entity Relationship Extraction with XAI
summary: Mathematical text understanding is a challenging task due to the presence of specialized entities and complex relationships between them. This study formulates mathematical problem interpretation as a Mathematical Entity Relation Extraction (MERE) task, where operands are treated as entities and operators as their relationships. Transformer-based models are applied to automatically extract these relations from mathematical text, with Bidirectional Encoder Representations from Transformers (BERT) achieving the best performance, reaching an accuracy of 99.39%. To enhance transparency and trust in the model's predictions, Explainable Artificial Intelligence (XAI) is incorporated using Shapley Additive Explanations (SHAP). The explainability analysis reveals how specific textual and mathematical features influence relation prediction, providing insights into feature importance and model behavior. By combining transformer-based learning, a task-specific dataset, and explainable modeling, this work offers an effective and interpretable framework for MERE, supporting future applications in automated problem solving, knowledge graph construction, and intelligent educational systems.

AI.tldr: They model understanding math word problems as extracting entities (operands) and relations (operators) using transformers (best: BERT ~99.4% accuracy), and explain model decisions with SHAP for transparency.
AI.motivation: Mathematical texts contain specialized symbols and complex relationships that are hard for machines to interpret. Existing NLP techniques struggle to accurately capture the structure of math problems, limiting automation in tasks like problem solving, building math knowledge graphs, and educational tools. The authors want a principled way to interpret math problem statements as structured relationships while also making model decisions transparent and trustworthy.
AI.method: They reformulate understanding a math problem as a Mathematical Entity Relation Extraction (MERE) task: operands (numbers, variables, quantities) are entities and operators (e.g., plus, minus, times, relational operators) are relations between them. They build a task-specific dataset of mathematical texts annotated with entities and relations. Several transformer-based models are trained to automatically extract these relations, with particular focus on BERT. To make the model interpretable, they apply Explainable AI via SHAP (Shapley Additive Explanations) to quantify how each textual and mathematical feature contributes to a predicted relation and to visualize feature importance.
AI.result: Among the transformer models, BERT performs best on the MERE task, achieving 99.39% accuracy in predicting mathematical relations between entities. The SHAP-based explainability analysis successfully highlights which tokens (words, symbols, numbers) are most influential for each prediction, showing consistent and plausible feature importance patterns that align with human intuition for math problem interpretation.
AI.conclusion: Representing math problem understanding as a relation extraction task and solving it with transformer models is highly effective, with BERT achieving near-perfect accuracy on their dataset. Integrating SHAP-based XAI provides interpretable insights into the model’s reasoning, helping to build trust and guide further improvements. This framework can serve as a foundation for downstream applications such as automated math problem solving, constructing mathematical knowledge graphs, and intelligent tutoring or educational systems that need both high accuracy and transparent decision-making.

title: Evaluation of Deontic Conditional Reasoning in Large Language Models: The Case of Wason's Selection Task
comment: To appear in the Proceedings of EACL 2026
summary: As large language models (LLMs) advance in linguistic competence, their reasoning abilities are gaining increasing attention. In humans, reasoning often performs well in domain specific settings, particularly in normative rather than purely formal contexts. Although prior studies have compared LLM and human reasoning, the domain specificity of LLM reasoning remains underexplored. In this study, we introduce a new Wason Selection Task dataset that explicitly encodes deontic modality to systematically distinguish deontic from descriptive conditionals, and use it to examine LLMs' conditional reasoning under deontic rules. We further analyze whether observed error patterns are better explained by confirmation bias (a tendency to seek rule-supporting evidence) or by matching bias (a tendency to ignore negation and select items that lexically match elements of the rule). Results show that, like humans, LLMs reason better with deontic rules and display matching-bias-like errors. Together, these findings suggest that the performance of LLMs varies systematically across rule types and that their error patterns can parallel well-known human biases in this paradigm.

AI.tldr: The paper studies how large language models reason with deontic (norm-based) versus descriptive conditionals using a new Wason Selection Task dataset, and finds that their performance patterns and biases resemble human reasoning.
AI.motivation: While LLMs are strong in language, their reasoning abilities—especially how they vary across domains and rule types—are less understood. In humans, reasoning is often domain-specific and better with normative (deontic) rules than with abstract descriptions. Existing comparisons of LLM and human reasoning have not systematically examined whether LLMs show similar domain specificity and human-like biases such as confirmation bias or matching bias. This paper aims to fill that gap.
AI.method: The authors build a specialized Wason Selection Task dataset that clearly distinguishes deontic (normative, obligation/permission) from descriptive conditionals by explicitly encoding deontic modality. They then prompt large language models with these tasks to test conditional reasoning under deontic versus descriptive rules. They analyze the models’ choices to determine both overall accuracy and the structure of errors, comparing them to patterns predicted by confirmation bias and matching bias.
AI.result: LLMs perform better on Wason Selection Tasks when the rules are deontic than when they are purely descriptive, paralleling human performance. The distribution of their errors aligns more with matching bias—favoring selections that lexically match rule elements and neglecting negations—than with confirmation bias alone. This indicates systematic variation in reasoning performance across rule types and characteristic, bias-like error patterns.
AI.conclusion: LLMs, like humans, exhibit domain-specific reasoning: they handle deontic conditionals better than descriptive ones and show matching-bias-like errors on the Wason task. These results imply that LLM reasoning is not uniform across contexts and that their error profiles can mirror established human cognitive biases, offering a more nuanced picture of LLM reasoning behavior and its similarities to human reasoning.

title: From Prompting to Preference Optimization: A Comparative Study of LLM-based Automated Essay Scoring
summary: Large language models (LLMs) have recently reshaped Automated Essay Scoring (AES), yet prior studies typically examine individual techniques in isolation, limiting understanding of their relative merits for English as a Second Language (L2) writing. To bridge this gap, we presents a comprehensive comparison of major LLM-based AES paradigms on IELTS Writing Task~2. On this unified benchmark, we evaluate four approaches: (i) encoder-based classification fine-tuning, (ii) zero- and few-shot prompting, (iii) instruction tuning and Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG), and (iv) Supervised Fine-Tuning combined with Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) and RAG. Our results reveal clear accuracy-cost-robustness trade-offs across methods, the best configuration, integrating k-SFT and RAG, achieves the strongest overall results with F1-Score 93%. This study offers the first unified empirical comparison of modern LLM-based AES strategies for English L2, promising potential in auto-grading writing tasks. Code is public at https://github.com/MinhNguyenDS/LLM_AES-EnL2

AI.tldr: The paper systematically compares several LLM-based approaches to automated scoring of IELTS essays written by English learners, finding that a supervised fine-tuned LLM combined with retrieval (RAG) works best, achieving very high scoring accuracy.
AI.motivation: Existing work on LLM-based Automated Essay Scoring usually evaluates single techniques in isolation and mostly on native or general English data, making it unclear which modern LLM strategies work best for English-as-a-second-language (L2) essays and what the trade-offs are between accuracy, cost, and robustness.
AI.method: The authors build a unified benchmark on IELTS Writing Task 2 essays and empirically compare four LLM-based AES paradigms: (i) encoder-based classification with standard fine-tuning, (ii) zero- and few-shot prompting of LLMs, (iii) instruction-tuned LLMs enhanced with Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG), and (iv) supervised fine-tuning (SFT) combined with Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) and RAG. They evaluate these configurations along accuracy, computational cost, and robustness.
AI.result: They observe distinct trade-offs between accuracy, inference cost, and robustness across the four paradigms. The best-performing setup is a k-shot supervised fine-tuned LLM integrated with RAG, which attains an F1-score of 93% on IELTS Task 2 L2 essays, outperforming other methods on overall performance.
AI.conclusion: A carefully designed LLM-based pipeline, particularly using supervised fine-tuning plus RAG, can achieve highly accurate automated scoring of L2 IELTS essays, outperforming simpler prompting or basic fine-tuning approaches. The work provides the first unified empirical comparison of modern LLM AES strategies for English L2 writing and indicates strong potential for reliable automatic grading in language testing contexts.

title: Beyond Rows to Reasoning: Agentic Retrieval for Multimodal Spreadsheet Understanding and Editing
summary: Recent advances in multimodal Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) enable Large Language Models (LLMs) to analyze enterprise spreadsheet workbooks containing millions of cells, cross-sheet dependencies, and embedded visual artifacts. However, state-of-the-art approaches exclude critical context through single-pass retrieval, lose data resolution through compression, and exceed LLM context windows through naive full-context injection, preventing reliable multi-step reasoning over complex enterprise workbooks. We introduce Beyond Rows to Reasoning (BRTR), a multimodal agentic framework for spreadsheet understanding that replaces single-pass retrieval with an iterative tool-calling loop, supporting end-to-end Excel workflows from complex analysis to structured editing. Supported by over 200 hours of expert human evaluation, BRTR achieves state-of-the-art performance across three frontier spreadsheet understanding benchmarks, surpassing prior methods by 25 percentage points on FRTR-Bench, 7 points on SpreadsheetLLM, and 32 points on FINCH. We evaluate five multimodal embedding models, identifying NVIDIA NeMo Retriever 1B as the top performer for mixed tabular and visual data, and vary nine LLMs. Ablation experiments confirm that the planner, retrieval, and iterative reasoning each contribute substantially, and cost analysis shows GPT-5.2 achieves the best efficiency-accuracy trade-off. Throughout all evaluations, BRTR maintains full auditability through explicit tool-call traces.

AI.tldr: BRTR is a multimodal, tool-using framework that lets LLMs reliably understand and edit large, complex enterprise spreadsheets via iterative retrieval and reasoning instead of single-pass context stuffing.
AI.motivation: Existing multimodal RAG methods for spreadsheets struggle with real enterprise workbooks that have millions of cells, cross-sheet formulas, and visual elements. Single-pass retrieval drops important context, compression loses fine-grained data needed for correct reasoning, and naive injection of all retrieved content overflows context windows. These limitations prevent accurate multi-step analysis and trustworthy automation of spreadsheet workflows, which are central to many enterprise processes.
AI.method: The authors propose Beyond Rows to Reasoning (BRTR), an agentic multimodal framework where an LLM orchestrates an iterative tool-calling loop instead of relying on one-shot retrieval. BRTR integrates specialized tools for retrieval over large spreadsheets, multimodal processing of tabular and visual artifacts, planning components for multi-step workflows, and structured editing tools for end-to-end Excel operations. They benchmark BRTR on three spreadsheet understanding datasets, evaluate five multimodal embedding models for mixed table-image retrieval, compare nine LLM backends, and run ablation studies removing components such as the planner, retrieval, and iterative reasoning. They also perform a cost-accuracy analysis across LLMs.
AI.result: BRTR sets new state-of-the-art results on three spreadsheet understanding benchmarks: +25 percentage points on FRTR-Bench, +7 points on SpreadsheetLLM, and +32 points on FINCH over prior methods. Among the evaluated embedding models, NVIDIA NeMo Retriever 1B performs best for mixed tabular and visual data. Across nine LLMs, GPT-5.2 provides the best balance between accuracy and cost. Ablation experiments show that each of the key components—planner, retrieval module, and iterative reasoning loop—substantially contributes to performance gains.
AI.conclusion: Iterative, tool-based multimodal RAG is more effective than single-pass or full-context approaches for complex spreadsheet understanding. BRTR demonstrates that combining planning, high-quality multimodal retrieval, and stepwise reasoning can robustly support realistic enterprise Excel workflows, from deep analysis to structured editing, while remaining auditable through explicit tool-call traces and cost-efficient with the right LLM and embedding model choices.

title: Speak in Context: Multilingual ASR with Speech Context Alignment via Contrastive Learning
summary: Automatic speech recognition (ASR) has benefited from advances in pretrained speech and language models, yet most systems remain constrained to monolingual settings and short, isolated utterances. While recent efforts in context-aware ASR show promise, two key challenges persist: limited multilingual support and the absence of principled alignment between speech and contextual representations.
