In this paper, we introduce a context-aware multilingual ASR framework that supports diverse languages and accents while preserving the modularity of pretrained models. Our approach combines a frozen speech encoder and a decoder-only language model via a lightweight projection module, allowing structured context prompts, including dialogue history and biasing words, to guide transcription. To improve interaction between speech and context, we employ a contrastive learning objective that aligns their representations in a shared embedding space. Evaluations on over 1,500 hours of real-world conversational speech across 11 languages and 5 English dialects show that contextual input consistently improves recognition quality. Contrastive alignment provides additional gains when applied to different context types, with an overall performance gain of over 5%. These results highlight the importance of both contextual modeling and cross-modal alignment in multilingual ASR.

AI.tldr: A context-aware multilingual ASR framework that integrates frozen speech encoders with decoder-only LMs via a projection module and contrastive alignment, improving recognition across 11 languages and 5 English dialects by over 5%.
AI.motivation: Most ASR systems are monolingual and process short, isolated utterances, with recent context-aware methods lacking broad multilingual support and principled alignment between speech and contextual information. The paper aims to address these gaps to better handle real-world multilingual conversational speech with rich context.
AI.method: The authors propose a modular framework that uses a frozen pretrained speech encoder and a decoder-only language model connected by a lightweight projection module. Structured context prompts (e.g., dialogue history, biasing words) are fed into the language model to guide transcription. They further introduce a contrastive learning objective that aligns speech and contextual representations in a shared embedding space, enhancing cross-modal interaction without fully retraining the underlying models.
AI.result: On more than 1,500 hours of real-world conversational data spanning 11 languages and 5 English dialects, adding contextual input consistently improves recognition performance. Applying the proposed contrastive alignment to different context types yields additional gains, leading to an overall word error rate reduction of over 5% compared to non-contextual or non-aligned baselines.
AI.conclusion: Contextual modeling and explicit cross-modal alignment between speech and textual context are both crucial for improving multilingual ASR. The proposed modular framework effectively leverages pretrained speech and language models with minimal architectural changes, scales across many languages and dialects, and delivers consistent accuracy improvements, underscoring the value of structured prompts and contrastive alignment in real-world conversational ASR.

title: KCLarity at SemEval-2026 Task 6: Encoder and Zero-Shot Approaches to Political Evasion Detection
summary: This paper describes the KCLarity team's participation in CLARITY, a shared task at SemEval 2026 on classifying ambiguity and evasion techniques in political discourse. We investigate two modelling formulations: (i) directly predicting the clarity label, and (ii) predicting the evasion label and deriving clarity through the task taxonomy hierarchy. We further explore several auxiliary training variants and evaluate decoder-only models in a zero-shot setting under the evasion-first formulation. Overall, the two formulations yield comparable performance. Among encoder-based models, RoBERTa-large achieves the strongest results on the public test set, while zero-shot GPT-5.2 generalises better on the hidden evaluation set.

AI.tldr: Paper reports KCLarity team’s SemEval 2026 CLARITY shared-task system for classifying ambiguity and evasion in political discourse, comparing two formulation strategies and multiple models, with RoBERTa-large best on public test and zero-shot GPT-5.2 best on hidden set.
AI.motivation: To effectively classify and understand ambiguity and evasion techniques in political discourse, within the CLARITY SemEval shared task, and to compare different problem formulations and model types for this task.
AI.method: They compare two task formulations: (i) directly predicting the clarity label, and (ii) first predicting an evasion label and then mapping it to clarity via the task’s taxonomy. They add several auxiliary training strategies and evaluate both encoder-based models (e.g., RoBERTa-large) and decoder-only LLMs (e.g., GPT-5.2) in a zero-shot setting using the evasion-first formulation.
AI.result: The direct clarity-prediction and evasion-first formulations achieve similar performance overall. Among encoder-based models, RoBERTa-large performs best on the public test set, while a zero-shot GPT-5.2 model generalises better to the hidden evaluation set.
AI.conclusion: Both clarity prediction formulations are viable for classifying ambiguity/evasion in political discourse, with encoder-based RoBERTa-large excelling on the public test and zero-shot GPT-5.2 showing superior generalisation on hidden data; formulation choice may be driven by practical or taxonomic considerations rather than clear performance differences.
